A cold front associated with a larger low pressure system move east through the Lower Ohio Valley on the evening of the 18th. This caused thunderstorms to move over southern Indiana and north central Kentucky, resulting in multiple reports of severe wind damage and five tornadoes across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS Storm Survey Team found EF-0 tornado damage in eastern Breckinridge County. The tornado touched down just north of Rosetta and continued northeastward toward Bewleyville. The most notable damage was done along Bewleyville-Rosetta Road where a small outbuilding was destroyed and strewn across a field. Tree damage was also observed with soft and hard wood trees being snapped. The tornado then lifted near Nelson Board Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a larger low pressure system move east through the Lower Ohio Valley on the evening of the 18th. This caused thunderstorms to move over southern Indiana and north central Kentucky, resulting in multiple reports of severe wind damage and five tornadoes across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the intersection of Spears Lane and St John Road. Damage was observed to a barn roof, which was completely lifted away. Debris was thrown mostly to the northeast, but in several varying direction including impaled wood planks in the nearby field. The tornado then crossed St John Road and struck a single residence with major roof loss and demolishing an outbuilding garage. The tornado continued to French Lane and Fuller Road where tree damage was observed, including debris of construction materials thrown into a nearby field. Damage continued across St John Church Road towards Kraft Road where isolated tree damage was observed. Some barn panel loss was observed along Thomas Road and tree damage continued toward Leasor Lane, as well as a single family residence having some minor roof panel damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate flooding occurred on the Ohio River. At the Paducah river gage, the river crested at 44.58 feet on the 2nd. Flood stage is 39 feet. Mainly bottomland and surrounding low-lying areas were flooded. Floodwaters crept into some back yards along Highway 1420 west of the city. The riverfront park in downtown Paducah was partially flooded but remained open to traffic.

A cold front associated with a larger low pressure system move east through the Lower Ohio Valley on the evening of the 18th. This caused thunderstorms to move over southern Indiana and north central Kentucky, resulting in multiple reports of severe wind damage and five tornadoes across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The National Weather Service in Louisville conducted a storm damage survey around West Point, Kentucky from a supercell storm last Friday night. The survey team found a somewhat  elevated and skipping EF0 tornado in and near West Point. The tornado began in a farmer`s field on the south side of town, with some trees twisted and snapped in the tallest portions of the trees.  The tornado uprooted and snapped a few trees in town as it moved to the northeast. Next it lifted a 20 by 12 shed roof onto a truck 15 yards away, in a northwesterly direction. A metal piece of the|roof went 35 yards upwind and was impaled on a stop sign. About a dozen homes had roof and siding damage. One homeowner showed a security camera video with a distinct cyclonic swirl, which was clearly seen around 1055 pm.||The skipping tornado crossed US Highway 31W with a few trees snapped or twisted and lifted near the Ohio Valley Dragway.||This is the same cell that produced a EF0 tornado in Breckinridge County. The EF0 tornado damage began in far northwestern Hardin County, and the survey team looked for damage in Bullitt County and Jefferson County. However, nothing more than a few downed trees could be located.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front associated with a larger low pressure system move east through the Lower Ohio Valley on the evening of the 18th. This caused thunderstorms to move over southern Indiana and north central Kentucky, resulting in multiple reports of severe wind damage and five tornadoes across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado was associated with the same parent thunderstorm as the Rineyville, KY tornado in Hardin County. This tornado redeveloped along Wilson Creek Road where several hard and soft wood trees were observed to be down in several directions, which included both snapped and uprooted trees. A carport roof was also partially blown away. The tornado then crossed Wilson Creek Road (Route 733) and affected several nearby residences with more tree damage to both hard and soft wood trees. No visible damage was observed toward the next hill side beyond the nearby farm property line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of accumulating snow shifted southeast across western Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours. Visibility was reduced to about one-half mile in the heaviest part of the band. Most places received one to two inches of snow, however a few isolated amounts of three inches were reported in the Pennyrile region.  Since pavement temperatures were above freezing, most of the accumulation was on grassy areas. However, some slushy accumulations on roadways caused locally hazardous travel. Storm total snowfall reports included 3 inches near Herndon (Christian County), 2 inches near Hopkinsville, 1.5 inches at Eddyville (Lyon County), and one inch at Paducah, Murray, and Fulton. The snow was caused by a surface low pressure system which organized over the southern Plains and then tracked along a stationary front that was stretched along the Ohio River.

An isolated semi-discrete supercell developed during the late afternoon hours in the warm sector of a surface low over south central Illinois. The storm developed in a marginally unstable but strongly sheared environment ahead of the trailing cold front. The cell produced large hail and severe wind gusts along a track from northeast Graves County through Marshall County to the south tip of Livingston County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of thunderstorms affected the region. The first round was during the evening, when small clusters of intense storms moved northeastward, producing isolated damaging wind events. These warm frontal storms were associated with a strengthening low-level jet and some cooling aloft. Late in the night, a line of thunderstorms extended along a cold front from southwest Indiana southwest through far southeast Missouri. As the line moved east, a few bowing segments within this line produced strong winds. Instability was  very limited, but strong winds aloft contributed to the production of isolated strong winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust to 69 mph was measured at the Henderson airport, just west of the city. In the city of Henderson, some small tree limbs were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken squall line of showers and thunderstorms produced scattered pockets of strong winds. The line moved rapidly east across western Kentucky. The line reformed a couple of times during its journey eastward. The strongly forced squall line was affected by very limited instability and moisture. Dew points were in the upper 50's ahead of the line. A very strong low level jet in excess of 80 knots resulted in some damaging wind gusts. Lightning was at times absent from the line. The line formed ahead of a strong cold front that trailed southward from a low pressure center passing north of the region. A very strong 500 mb shortwave pivoted east-northeast from the southern Plains, further enhancing the convection despite weak instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the second half of March, precipitation across the region was generally minimal to nil, which allowed the forest litter and dead trees/branches to become very dry. This helped set the stage for a high fire danger situation as rapidly deepening low pressure passed to the west on March 30th. Strong southerly flow ahead of the system led to wind gusts of around 40 mph at many locations by early afternoon. In addition, a bubble of very warm and dry air over the Southeast US was pulled northward by the low-level jet and further dried due to downsloping effects off of the Great Smoky and Cumberland mountains. The combination of strong winds, very warm temperatures, dry fuels, and low relative humidities were very favorable for rapid fire spread and a number of small wildfires ended up breaking out across eastern Kentucky.||     After a brief lull in the gusts during the evening of the 30th, the most intense winds with this storm system roared across eastern Kentucky from midnight to 4 AM on the 31st along and just ahead of a line of decaying showers moving in from the west. Wind gusts peaked in the 40 to 60 mph range across much of the forecast area during this time frame. Numerous power outages resulted, especially near/southwest of KY-15. Among the hardest hit was Owsley County, where over half the county lost power for a few hours overnight when the powerful winds blew a large tree down and damaged infrastructure near a substation. The winds also blew trees down onto power lines leading to downed lines and snapped power poles. A few instances of significant roof damage were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A local utility company reported that multiple trees were blown down onto power lines and broke a 3-phase power pole along KY-3483.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on county roads at least a few times across the county. The locations included the Lynn Grove area, a few miles southeast of Murray, and seven miles northwest of Hamlin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broken squall line of showers and thunderstorms produced scattered pockets of strong winds. The line moved rapidly east across western Kentucky. The line reformed a couple of times during its journey eastward. The strongly forced squall line was affected by very limited instability and moisture. Dew points were in the upper 50's ahead of the line. A very strong low level jet in excess of 80 knots resulted in some damaging wind gusts. Lightning was at times absent from the line. The line formed ahead of a strong cold front that trailed southward from a low pressure center passing north of the region. A very strong 500 mb shortwave pivoted east-northeast from the southern Plains, further enhancing the convection despite weak instability.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and a couple of power poles and lines were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the second half of March, precipitation across the region was generally minimal to nil, which allowed the forest litter and dead trees/branches to become very dry. This helped set the stage for a high fire danger situation as rapidly deepening low pressure passed to the west on March 30th. Strong southerly flow ahead of the system led to wind gusts of around 40 mph at many locations by early afternoon. In addition, a bubble of very warm and dry air over the Southeast US was pulled northward by the low-level jet and further dried due to downsloping effects off of the Great Smoky and Cumberland mountains. The combination of strong winds, very warm temperatures, dry fuels, and low relative humidities were very favorable for rapid fire spread and a number of small wildfires ended up breaking out across eastern Kentucky.||     After a brief lull in the gusts during the evening of the 30th, the most intense winds with this storm system roared across eastern Kentucky from midnight to 4 AM on the 31st along and just ahead of a line of decaying showers moving in from the west. Wind gusts peaked in the 40 to 60 mph range across much of the forecast area during this time frame. Numerous power outages resulted, especially near/southwest of KY-15. Among the hardest hit was Owsley County, where over half the county lost power for a few hours overnight when the powerful winds blew a large tree down and damaged infrastructure near a substation. The winds also blew trees down onto power lines leading to downed lines and snapped power poles. A few instances of significant roof damage were also reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The local utility company reported multiple broken power poles and numerous small power outages due to trees falling on power lines. Broken poles were located along Hounchell Rd. and Liberty Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough axis and surface cold front moved from the Plains through the Ohio Valley on March 11-12, 2022. A coupled upper level jet structure resulted in strong divergence in the upper levels of the atmosphere, which induced strong ascent in the low to mid levels as well. Plunging temperatures into the 20s Friday evening into early Saturday morning behind the cold front resulted in impactful snow accumulations. The presence of weak conditional symmetric instability allowed heavy banded snow to develop over portions of central and eastern Kentucky. Heavy snowfall rates accumulated on roadways and interstates, snarling traffic and resulting in significant societal impact. Up to half a foot of snow fell over portions of Kentucky east of Interstate 65.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow accumulation of 5 inches was reported in Warren County early Saturday March 12, 2022. Heavy banded snow with high snowfall rates resulted in treacherous travel conditions on Interstate 65.

Afternoon heat indices peaked around 105 degrees. Specific readings included 106 at Henderson, 105 at Madisonville, and 109 at Elkton. The heat occurred under a strong southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front over the central Plains.

Multiple waves of showers and thunderstorms moved across central Kentucky on August 6th and 7th as an exceptionally moist environment promoted the development of precipitation across the region. Precipitable water values approaching 2 inches remained over the area both days, and a lack of significant large-scale forcing kept storm motions fairly slow. As a result, multiple instances of flash flooding were observed across the area in locations where thunderstorms lingered for several hours. Additionally, while effective shear was weak, there was sufficient instability (around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE on average) for isolated severe convection. On August 6th, quarter-size hail was observed in Edmonson County near a maximum in mid-level lapse rates, while isolated wind damage reports were observed on both the 6th and the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were more than 20 trees and power lines down across Jim Roberts Community Park. A barn was destroyed just north of the park along Ditmore Ford Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved throughout the month of August. The month began with extreme drought along the Tennessee border and severe drought across the remainder of far west Kentucky, mainly west of the Land Between the Lakes area. By the end of the month, most areas were lifted out of moderate drought into the abnormally dry category. Improvement in crop status and fire danger was rather temporary. A return to dry conditions would commence in September.

Scattered thunderstorms developed during the heat of the day ahead of a cold front. One of these storms produced a damaging microburst, and another caused flash flooding of roads. The cold front trailed southwest from a low pressure center over the Great Lakes region across northern Illinois and central Missouri.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were down. Significant damage was reported at Iron Hill Campground just southwest of Kuttawa. A woman was briefly trapped, but another resident freed her and she was not injured. Some homes and golf carts in the campground were damaged by fallen trees and limbs.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front brought precipitation to Simpson County on April 11 and 12th. This caused West Fork Drake Creek to rise and flow over a bridge on Saddler Ford Road. A driver tried to cross the bridge, but flowing water pushed his vehicle off the bridge, killing him.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At approximately 7:55 PM CDT, Kentucky State Police received a call from a 41 year old male. He stated he was trying to drive over Saddler Ford Bridge, but his 2013 Ford pick-up was being swept off the bridge. The emergency manager later stated that the driver disregarded warning signs and barricades that were set up before the bridge warning drivers of the flowing water over the bridge. The man's body was found around a mile downstream the following day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a high pressure center sitting east of the state, Kentucky, along with much of the Deep South found themselves in a strong return flow pattern. This consisted of S to SW winds which were able to pump in ample moisture and warm air from the Gulf of Mexico. However, with no boundaries or airmass changes nearby, the flow aloft was not very strong. High moisture and weak flow materialized into slow moving showers and thunderstorms throughout the day on Saturday. As locations saw rain persist throughout the day, isolated instances of flooding and flash flooding were reported across portions of Rockcastle, Powell, Laurel, Jackson, and Estill Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported on KY-1505 near the intersection with KY-25.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front laid along the lower Ohio Valley on the afternoon of August 10th and resulted in diurnally driven showers and thunderstorms. The combination of slow moving and repetitive storms making their way through eastern Kentucky, an area that had been slammed with rain the day before, led to instances of flash flooding in Boyd and Lawrence Counties. Aside from minor flooding on local roads, a few homes were surrounded by water and one swift water rescue was conducted to save a citizen who found themselves stuck within the floodwaters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Ashland, flooding was observed along West New Buckley Road with several yards and basements inundated with water. A water rescue was conducted along Beach Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On August 9th and 10th, a quasi-stationary surface front meandered along the Ohio River, serving as a focus for the development of daytime showers and thunderstorms. Immediately south of the surface boundary, an unusually moist environment was present, with precipitable water values exceeding 2 inches across central Kentucky. As a result, as modest instability developed during the afternoon hours on the 9th, scattered showers and thunderstorms formed across Kentucky, with one cell growing strong enough to produce isolated wind damage across Trimble County. Then, on the afternoon of August 10th, widespread showers and thunderstorms once again developed across most of central Kentucky, with storms producing heavy rainfall amounts thanks to weak steering flow (slow storm motion) and the aforementioned available moisture. Flash flooding was observed during the afternoon hours across several locations, with the most significant flooding being observed in Franklin County and Casey County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down across Kentucky 36.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very slow-moving areas of thunderstorms crossed western Kentucky during the overnight hours into the early morning hours. These storms dumped up to 5 inches of rain in about 6 hours. Isolated pockets of flash flooding occurred with these excessive rain totals. The storms were associated with a weak cold front that extended from Lake Michigan south into the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six to eight inches of water was flowing across Highway 813 near Mortons Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Persistent clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms moved southeast across portions of western Kentucky. The storms first moved across the Ohio River into the Henderson and Owensboro areas early in the evening. The last of the severe storms exited the Pennyrile region of west Kentucky during the overnight hours. The storms initially formed over Illinois and Indiana along the residual outflow boundary left by an early morning thunderstorm complex. The atmosphere was very unstable during the late afternoon and early evening, when peak mixed-layer capes approached 4000 J/kg. Once the storms formed an organized cold pool, new thunderstorms developed on the western flank of the storm complex as an unstable southwest wind flow was lifted over the cold pool.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs and several trees were down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon hours on August 21st, a mid-level trough and associated surface cold front entered central Kentucky from the northwest. Ahead of the surface front, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed, with most storms remaining sub-severe. Overall, instability was relatively modest, with between 1000 and 1500 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE across southern and central Kentucky. Environmental shear was also modest, leading to mainly single cell and multicell clusters of thunderstorms. Nevertheless, a strong cell over Russell County was able to intensify enough to produce isolated wind damage across portions of the county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs fell on Highway 76 in Eli. A barn also had its roof metal peeled back.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours on August 3rd, showers and thunderstorms developed over north central Tennessee, eventually drifting northeast into south central Kentucky. Afternoon temperatures in the low 90s and dewpoints in the low-to-mid 70s contributed to the development of an unstable environment, with 2000-3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE over the region. However, environmental shear was weak, limiting the potential for widespread strong or severe thunderstorms. However, one particularly strong storm was able to grow enough to produce isolated wind damage across Clinton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple trees down and a porch roof blown off on Old Monticello Road near Smith Chapel Church.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed across parts of far western Kentucky during the overnight hours and burned off early in the morning. The dense fog was mainly along and west of a line from Benton to Murray. Visibility was at or below one-quarter mile. The fog formed under a light northeast wind flow from high pressure over the upper Mississippi Valley.

Multiple waves of showers and thunderstorms moved across central Kentucky on August 6th and 7th as an exceptionally moist environment promoted the development of precipitation across the region. Precipitable water values approaching 2 inches remained over the area both days, and a lack of significant large-scale forcing kept storm motions fairly slow. As a result, multiple instances of flash flooding were observed across the area in locations where thunderstorms lingered for several hours. Additionally, while effective shear was weak, there was sufficient instability (around 2000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE on average) for isolated severe convection. On August 6th, quarter-size hail was observed in Edmonson County near a maximum in mid-level lapse rates, while isolated wind damage reports were observed on both the 6th and the 7th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private bridge, made out of gravel, and culverts were washed away in eastern Anderson County. The exact location is not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed ahead of a low pressure system moving through the Ohio Valley.

Drought conditions improved throughout the month of August. The month began with extreme drought along the Tennessee border and severe drought across the remainder of far west Kentucky, mainly west of the Land Between the Lakes area. By the end of the month, most areas were lifted out of moderate drought into the abnormally dry category. Improvement in crop status and fire danger was rather temporary. A return to dry conditions would commence in September.

Persistent clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms moved southeast across portions of western Kentucky. The storms first moved across the Ohio River into the Henderson and Owensboro areas early in the evening. The last of the severe storms exited the Pennyrile region of west Kentucky during the overnight hours. The storms initially formed over Illinois and Indiana along the residual outflow boundary left by an early morning thunderstorm complex. The atmosphere was very unstable during the late afternoon and early evening, when peak mixed-layer capes approached 4000 J/kg. Once the storms formed an organized cold pool, new thunderstorms developed on the western flank of the storm complex as an unstable southwest wind flow was lifted over the cold pool.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree limb fell on an occupied camper at the Diamond Lake campground near West Louisville. There were no injuries, but people were trapped in the camper. The camper was not a total loss. Elsewhere in the county, there were several tree limbs on power lines. Most of the trees and power lines were around the St. Joseph and West Louisville area of the county.

A tree went through the windshield of a CSX freight train locomotive, injuring two crew members. All of the damage was near U.S. Highway 41 from Highway 407 to Highway 800.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two very slow-moving areas of thunderstorms crossed western Kentucky during the overnight hours into the early morning hours. These storms dumped up to 5 inches of rain in about 6 hours. Isolated pockets of flash flooding occurred with these excessive rain totals. The storms were associated with a weak cold front that extended from Lake Michigan south into the lower Ohio Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous streets were closed in Central City, including South 2nd Street and Broad Street. A water rescue was conducted on Taylor Road. A car was submerged. At least three feet of water were inside a home. Homes on Pattontown Drive in Central City were evacuated. Flooding was reported on Highway 175 near the interchange with the West Kentucky Parkway near Graham. Two separate rainfall totals over 6 inches were measured in Central City. Totals of 6.02 and 6.39 inches were measured by separate personal weather stations in Central City between midnight and 7 AM.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the Ohio Valley as an upper level disturbance moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A car was stranded in high water and the occupants had to be rescued.

Thunderstorms broke out across east Kentucky May 26th thanks to a mature low pressure system moving into the Ohio Valley from the west. This system initially pulled a warm front through eastern Kentucky in the evening, and most of the severe convection was associated with the approach and passage of this warm front. A cold front was then dragged across the region overnight and into the morning. Instability was not overly impressive going into this event, around 1000-1500 J/kg of CAPE. However, storms that formed were able to take advantage of the roughly 40 kts of effective shear over the area and organize into more multicellular and linear structures. The limited CAPE profile meant a more limited hail threat, leaving strong to damaging wind gusts as the primary threat, though tornadoes were not able to be ruled out. Even though storm motions were fairly swift, given the abundant moisture associated with this system, there was also a threat for flash flooding if any training set up or an area was hit multiple times. Several warnings, including Severe Thunderstorm, Flash Flood, and Tornado, were issued that evening, mainly across our most western and southwestern counties. Multiple reports of damaging wind leading to downed trees were received across the same areas, along with a couple of reports of water flowing over roadways. There were some wind reports within the Tornado Warnings, but no tornadoes were confirmed in the JKL area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over Route 66 following thunderstorms with heavy rain. It was reported that the water had receded prior to 6:30 PM EDT (5:30 PM EST).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave trough was situated over the Upper Midwest Sunday night (31st) into Monday morning (1st). At the surface, a strong occluded low was also present. From this low and occlusion, a warm front extended to the southeast, lifting through the Commonwealth during the same time frame. A MCS developed in the Upper Mississippi Valley and continued to push southeast towards the Commonwealth during the morning hours. This prompted SPC to put portions of northern and eastern KY in a slight risk for severe weather throughout the day. Ironically, we did not see any severe weather out of this system - however, showers that developed during the morning hours ahead of the MCS were a different story. These showers developed over the eastern portion of the state, and impacted areas that were already heavily saturated from the devastating rains that occurred the week before. More so, the convection was moving parallel to the mean flow, which meant that many of these showers continued to train over the same locations for an extended period of time. This prompted flash flood warnings, and led to impacts in Martin, Pike, Letcher, Floyd, and Knott counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media reports water flowing over Big Branch Ball Road near the community of Emmalena.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was situated over Iowa/Illinois/Missouri during the day of the 18th, with a  warm front extended eastward and across and just north of the Bluegrass State. SPC had placed much of our area in a marginal risk of severe weather early in the day, before upgrading to a slight category during a morning update. A secondary low also developed and moved into the state for the afternoon, creating a secondary warm front boundary - along which lift and all modes of severe weather were possible, with the best chances for wind and hail. There was enough low level sheer that an isolated tornado couldn't be ruled out. As a shortwave trough also approached eastern KY throughout the day, along with an MCS to our west, the atmosphere was primed for severe weather to occur across the southeastern portions of the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several powerlines reported down near 461 Shopville Rd. in Shopville, Kentucky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between July 25th and July 30th, 2022, several complexes of training thunderstorms developed south of I-64 and brought heavy rain, deadly flash flooding, and devastating river flooding to eastern Kentucky and central Appalachia. These thunderstorms, at times, caused rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr across complex terrain that led to widespread devastating impacts. While it did not rain continuously during this 4-day stretch, the overwhelming amounts of rain and resultant flooding led to 39 deaths and widespread catastrophic damage. Entire homes and parts of some communities were swept away by flood waters, leading to costly damage to infrastructure in the region. Over 600 helicopter rescues and countless swift water rescues by boat were needed to evacuate people who were trapped by the quickly-rising flood waters. In total, 24 Flash Flood Warnings were issued between July 26th and July 30th. Between the evening of July 27th and the mid-morning hours on July 28th (the peak of the event), 13 warnings were issued, 3 of which were upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency.||Radar-based rainfall estimates suggest that upwards of 14-16 of rain fell during this 5-day period in a narrow swath, with many more locations receiving 6-10 of rain. Most of this rain fell during the night of July 27th into the morning of July 28th, which is when the most devastating impacts were felt. The highest totals occurred across an axis that stretches from northern Clay and southern Owsley counties, east through southern Breathitt and northern Leslie counties, into Perry, Knott and Letcher counties. The highest rainfall total report was from southern Knott County, where 14.00 fell between July 25th and July 29th. This site, a cooperative (COOP) observer at Carr Creek Lake, reported 6.71 from 7am July 28th to 7am July 29th, following a report of 6.50 at that site on the previous day. Another COOP site in Buckhorn reported 8.00 of rain for the 24-hour period ending 7am on July 28th. The rainfall total in Buckhorn from July 25th to July 29th was 11.76. These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Runoff from heavy rains eventually found their way to to the Right Fork of Beaver Creek in southwest Floyd County, and Beaver Creek near Martin, leading to multiple reports of flooding in communities near the water. Some reports included:||At 8:30 AM EST - Pictures show the Marathon Gas Station along KY-7 (Main Street) in Wayland inundated by flood waters. Fuel pumps were completely submerged. |At 3:14 PM EST - Video footage showed a water rescue by the bridge on KY-7 near Maddy's Pizza in Hueysville.|At 4:00 PM EST - A picture on social media showed water up to the windows on trailer homes in Hueysville.|At 4:19 PM EST - The Garret community was reported to be inundated by several feet of flood waters from the Right Fork Beaver Creek.|At 6:28 PM EST - The Martin Fire Department reported flood waters over Brawley Street in Martin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a high pressure center sitting east of the state, Kentucky, along with much of the Deep South found themselves in a strong return flow pattern. This consisted of S to SW winds which were able to pump in ample moisture and warm air from the Gulf of Mexico. However, with no boundaries or airmass changes nearby, the flow aloft was not very strong. High moisture and weak flow materialized into slow moving showers and thunderstorms throughout the day on Saturday. As locations saw rain persist throughout the day, isolated instances of flooding and flash flooding were reported across portions of Rockcastle, Powell, Laurel, Jackson, and Estill Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported over the road on Adams Ridge Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was present across the Deep South moving towards the Tennessee Valley throughout the day, with WPC analyzing a secondary stationary boundary developing along the Ohio River by the evening. Overall forcing was not strong across the state of Kentucky, however. Most of the state remained dry throughout the day, however a few isolated showers and thunderstorms developed across the far southeast part of the state into the evening. The storm across Bell county was the strongest, and with very little flow aloft, the storm did not move much. It continued to sit over the county for a couple of hours, eventually leading to a handful of flash flooding reports. In addition, winds from the storm led to trees being downed onto KY-119 near the community of Miracle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down along Highway 119 in and around Tanyard Hill, where flash flooding was also ongoing. Trees and flooding were blocking the road in places.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and storms fired during the afternoon and evening of the 19th in the vicinity of a wavering frontal boundary over eastern Kentucky.  High amounts of instability and ample shear favored splitting supercells. While there were many reports of dime to quarter-sized hail, two particularly intense storms produced up to egg-size hail near Jackson and Stanton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public shared a picture of golf to egg-sized (1.75 to 2.00 inch) hail from the vicinity of the Jackson Ace Hardware.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between July 25th and July 30th, 2022, several complexes of training thunderstorms developed south of I-64 and brought heavy rain, deadly flash flooding, and devastating river flooding to eastern Kentucky and central Appalachia. These thunderstorms, at times, caused rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr across complex terrain that led to widespread devastating impacts. While it did not rain continuously during this 4-day stretch, the overwhelming amounts of rain and resultant flooding led to 39 deaths and widespread catastrophic damage. Entire homes and parts of some communities were swept away by flood waters, leading to costly damage to infrastructure in the region. Over 600 helicopter rescues and countless swift water rescues by boat were needed to evacuate people who were trapped by the quickly-rising flood waters. In total, 24 Flash Flood Warnings were issued between July 26th and July 30th. Between the evening of July 27th and the mid-morning hours on July 28th (the peak of the event), 13 warnings were issued, 3 of which were upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency.||Radar-based rainfall estimates suggest that upwards of 14-16 of rain fell during this 5-day period in a narrow swath, with many more locations receiving 6-10 of rain. Most of this rain fell during the night of July 27th into the morning of July 28th, which is when the most devastating impacts were felt. The highest totals occurred across an axis that stretches from northern Clay and southern Owsley counties, east through southern Breathitt and northern Leslie counties, into Perry, Knott and Letcher counties. The highest rainfall total report was from southern Knott County, where 14.00 fell between July 25th and July 29th. This site, a cooperative (COOP) observer at Carr Creek Lake, reported 6.71 from 7am July 28th to 7am July 29th, following a report of 6.50 at that site on the previous day. Another COOP site in Buckhorn reported 8.00 of rain for the 24-hour period ending 7am on July 28th. The rainfall total in Buckhorn from July 25th to July 29th was 11.76. These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.

Residents in the area were evacuating multiple homes south of KY-197.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was present across the Deep South moving towards the Tennessee Valley throughout the day, with WPC analyzing a secondary stationary boundary developing along the Ohio River by the evening. Overall forcing was not strong across the state of Kentucky, however. Most of the state remained dry throughout the day, however a few isolated showers and thunderstorms developed across the far southeast part of the state into the evening. The storm across Bell county was the strongest, and with very little flow aloft, the storm did not move much. It continued to sit over the county for a couple of hours, eventually leading to a handful of flash flooding reports. In addition, winds from the storm led to trees being downed onto KY-119 near the community of Miracle.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along Highway 221 near the Kettle Island community, causing the road to be blocked in places.

Some of the reports included:||11:00 PM EST - Pictures on social media show flash flooding around the Big Branch Baptist Church. |11:56 PM EST - Broadcast media reported street flash flooding leaving KY-899 impassable in front of the Holly Hills Shopping Center. |12:38 AM EST - 4 children, ranging from 1 to 8 years old, perished in the flood waters of Montgomery and Troublesome Creeks after being swept away from their parents. The reports go on to state that it was a matter of minutes before the family was forced to escape to the roof of their mobile home once water started pouring in. They were not on the roof very long before they realized the mobile home was not stable, and they swam to a nearby tree. Strong currents in the water ripped the children away from the parent's grasp. It was around 8 hours before the parents were rescued. 2 of the 4 children were found deceased on Thursday (7/28). EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was in place across the state during the morning hours on May 6th, setting the stage for increased moisture and warm air into the region. By the afternoon, a nearby low pressure system began to occlude, furthering the lift and instability across the state. Showers and thunderstorms developed across much of the region throughout the day, becoming strong to severe by mid-day. Flooding and wind damage was reported throughout the afternoon and into the evening. A tornado was also reported and confirmed near General Burnside in Pulaski County during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A motorist was trapped by flash flood waters at the intersection of Highway 70 and Charles Elgin Road near Woodstock. A water rescue had to be performed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between July 25th and July 30th, 2022, several complexes of training thunderstorms developed south of I-64 and brought heavy rain, deadly flash flooding, and devastating river flooding to eastern Kentucky and central Appalachia. These thunderstorms, at times, caused rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr across complex terrain that led to widespread devastating impacts. While it did not rain continuously during this 4-day stretch, the overwhelming amounts of rain and resultant flooding led to 39 deaths and widespread catastrophic damage. Entire homes and parts of some communities were swept away by flood waters, leading to costly damage to infrastructure in the region. Over 600 helicopter rescues and countless swift water rescues by boat were needed to evacuate people who were trapped by the quickly-rising flood waters. In total, 24 Flash Flood Warnings were issued between July 26th and July 30th. Between the evening of July 27th and the mid-morning hours on July 28th (the peak of the event), 13 warnings were issued, 3 of which were upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency.||Radar-based rainfall estimates suggest that upwards of 14-16 of rain fell during this 5-day period in a narrow swath, with many more locations receiving 6-10 of rain. Most of this rain fell during the night of July 27th into the morning of July 28th, which is when the most devastating impacts were felt. The highest totals occurred across an axis that stretches from northern Clay and southern Owsley counties, east through southern Breathitt and northern Leslie counties, into Perry, Knott and Letcher counties. The highest rainfall total report was from southern Knott County, where 14.00 fell between July 25th and July 29th. This site, a cooperative (COOP) observer at Carr Creek Lake, reported 6.71 from 7am July 28th to 7am July 29th, following a report of 6.50 at that site on the previous day. Another COOP site in Buckhorn reported 8.00 of rain for the 24-hour period ending 7am on July 28th. The rainfall total in Buckhorn from July 25th to July 29th was 11.76. These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State troopers reported running water and debris across KY-52 at the intersection of KY-2453.

These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple instances of flash flooding occurred in SW Owsley County.||At 10:00 PM EST - Pictures on social media show significant flash flood damange along Buffalo Creek, including a home washed off it's foundation and destroyed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a stationary boundary well to our northwest, and high pressure centered to our southeast, KY found itself in a weak return flow pattern. Winds were relatively light, with strong subsidence aloft thanks to an upper level ridge in place. Severe weather was not expected during the day, with most of the eastern conus in just a general thunderstorm risk. That being said, a few showers and thunderstorms did develop. Without a strong steering flow, the shower was slow to move, and therefore ended up leading to an isolated report of flooding in Perry County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported on the roadway on Briar Fork Circle Road near Avawam. 911 dispatch responded to the report. The flooding was shown in a video that had been posted on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary was draped just north of the Ohio River, which allowed for the development of generally isolated showers and thunderstorms across the state throughout the day. One of these storms developed near the eastern TN border in Whitley County, and eventually was warned. This storm did end up producing an insolated instance of wind damage, with one tree being reported down near the community of Louden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down near the 2700 Block of Louden-Meadow Creek Rd near the community of Louden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system was in place across the Upper Mississippi Valley, with all attention for severe weather concentrated there for the day. SPC had all of eastern Kentucky in just a general thunderstorm risk, with increasing risk levels as you headed farther northwest towards the main storm system. That being said, with high pressure to our southeast, Kentucky was in a warm and moist airmass with decent SW flow into the state. Showers and thunderstorms developed throughout the day, mainly in an area oriented from SW to NE. These expanded to eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours. Storms ended up producing generally isolated wind damage in Perry and Montgomery counties. The heavy rain also caused some urban flooding in Hazard, KY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and onto Highway 11 in Levee. Power outages were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and storms fired during the afternoon and evening of the 19th in the vicinity of a wavering frontal boundary over eastern Kentucky.  High amounts of instability and ample shear favored splitting supercells. While there were many reports of dime to quarter-sized hail, two particularly intense storms produced up to egg-size hail near Jackson and Stanton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An individual near Gray Hawk reported quarter-sized (1.00 inch) hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving from Indiana through the upper Ohio Valley dragged a cold front toward the Bluegrass state over the afternoon and evening of May 3rd. Showers and storms fired up across eastern Kentucky in the late afternoon and early evening hours ahead of the front as instability built. A couple of storms became severe late in the evening as the front neared eastern Kentucky's doorstep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to have been blown down along KY-1004 northeast of Mount Vernon, near Orlando.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was in place across the state during the morning hours on May 6th, setting the stage for increased moisture and warm air into the region. By the afternoon, a nearby low pressure system began to occlude, furthering the lift and instability across the state. Showers and thunderstorms developed across much of the region throughout the day, becoming strong to severe by mid-day. Flooding and wind damage was reported throughout the afternoon and into the evening. A tornado was also reported and confirmed near General Burnside in Pulaski County during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing over Highway 52 near Irvine.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed across the Ohio Valley as an upper level disturbance moved through the region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge was washed out on Little Sugar Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms broke out across east Kentucky May 26th thanks to a mature low pressure system moving into the Ohio Valley from the west. This system initially pulled a warm front through eastern Kentucky in the evening, and most of the severe convection was associated with the approach and passage of this warm front. A cold front was then dragged across the region overnight and into the morning. Instability was not overly impressive going into this event, around 1000-1500 J/kg of CAPE. However, storms that formed were able to take advantage of the roughly 40 kts of effective shear over the area and organize into more multicellular and linear structures. The limited CAPE profile meant a more limited hail threat, leaving strong to damaging wind gusts as the primary threat, though tornadoes were not able to be ruled out. Even though storm motions were fairly swift, given the abundant moisture associated with this system, there was also a threat for flash flooding if any training set up or an area was hit multiple times. Several warnings, including Severe Thunderstorm, Flash Flood, and Tornado, were issued that evening, mainly across our most western and southwestern counties. Multiple reports of damaging wind leading to downed trees were received across the same areas, along with a couple of reports of water flowing over roadways. There were some wind reports within the Tornado Warnings, but no tornadoes were confirmed in the JKL area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree damage, including uprooted or snapped trees, was reported at this location.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was slowly pushing through the state on Saturday, July 2. Warm and humid conditions ahead of the front led to destabilization, and the development of strong storms with excessive rain and frequent cloud to ground lightning. Several of these storms out-preformed the rest, with higher cores and stronger winds. This resulted in four severe thunderstorm warnings being issued throughout the evening across southeast Kentucky. Unfortunately, only one of these storms actually resulted in realized severe winds at the surface, near Jackson in Breathitt County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large tree limbs were reported down in the road on Washington Avenue in Jackson.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front set up just west of Kentucky and became stalled throughout the day on May 21. Ahead of this cold front, Kentucky was experiencing warm, humid, and unstable conditions across the region. By the afternoon, this led to the development of multiple lines of showers and thunderstorms. The general trend was for these storms to begin dissipating the farther east they went, out of the most unstable air. Best instability, along with the most potent line of storms, were located to our west across western and central Kentucky, nearer to the frontal boundary. However, this didn't deter an individual storm from continuing ahead of the main line and impacting Rowan and Fleming Counties, resulting in some wind damage in Rowan County as it moved through.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and a power line were reported down on Big Perry Road, just west of the Haldeman community.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain occurred in portions of northeast Kentucky on May 14, leading to swollen creeks and streams across the area. While no flash flood reports were received, there was a fatality reported in Rowan County. According to the county EM and rescue squad, an 80-year-old man was on a utility vehicle with his wife, attempting to cross a swollen creek along Bullfork Road. The utility vehicle reportedly got stuck, and the man got off the vehicle and into the high waters to free the vehicle and get to the other side. The current of the water knocked him off his feet, and he was swept away down the creek. Rescue workers were able to get to him, and take him to a nearby medical center - however, he was later pronounced dead.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: According to an article from LEX18 news in Lexington: Kentucky State Police say they were first called out in reference to a drowning on Bullfork Road in Rowan County. They say an 80-year-old man tried to cross a swollen tributary in a utility vehicle. He got out of the vehicle and tried to get to the other side of the tributary. While crossing, troops say he was swept away in the current. He was rescued and taken to the regional medical center, where he was pronounced dead.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between July 25th and July 30th, 2022, several complexes of training thunderstorms developed south of I-64 and brought heavy rain, deadly flash flooding, and devastating river flooding to eastern Kentucky and central Appalachia. These thunderstorms, at times, caused rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr across complex terrain that led to widespread devastating impacts. While it did not rain continuously during this 4-day stretch, the overwhelming amounts of rain and resultant flooding led to 39 deaths and widespread catastrophic damage. Entire homes and parts of some communities were swept away by flood waters, leading to costly damage to infrastructure in the region. Over 600 helicopter rescues and countless swift water rescues by boat were needed to evacuate people who were trapped by the quickly-rising flood waters. In total, 24 Flash Flood Warnings were issued between July 26th and July 30th. Between the evening of July 27th and the mid-morning hours on July 28th (the peak of the event), 13 warnings were issued, 3 of which were upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency.||Radar-based rainfall estimates suggest that upwards of 14-16 of rain fell during this 5-day period in a narrow swath, with many more locations receiving 6-10 of rain. Most of this rain fell during the night of July 27th into the morning of July 28th, which is when the most devastating impacts were felt. The highest totals occurred across an axis that stretches from northern Clay and southern Owsley counties, east through southern Breathitt and northern Leslie counties, into Perry, Knott and Letcher counties. The highest rainfall total report was from southern Knott County, where 14.00 fell between July 25th and July 29th. This site, a cooperative (COOP) observer at Carr Creek Lake, reported 6.71 from 7am July 28th to 7am July 29th, following a report of 6.50 at that site on the previous day. Another COOP site in Buckhorn reported 8.00 of rain for the 24-hour period ending 7am on July 28th. The rainfall total in Buckhorn from July 25th to July 29th was 11.76. These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Bell County dispatch reported that Balkan Road off of US-119 was closed due to high water in the community of Callaway.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding across portions of Perry County persisted into areal flooding. In addition the North Fork Kentucky River began rising. It reached minor flood stage (20 feet) by 12:30 PM EST on the 28th, and then went on to crest at 25.76 feet by 10:45 PM EST. It then fell to below flood stage by 5 AM EST the following morning.||Some additional flooding reports received for Perry County between 7/28 and 7/29 include:||At 11:58 AM EST (7/28) - KY-451 was inundated by the North Fork of the Kentucky River and Campbell Creek in the Krypton community. |At 2:00 PM EST - PIctures from broadcast media show flood waters from the North Fork of the Kentucky River inundating the intersection of KY-458 and KY-28 just outside of Chavies. |At 6:56 PM EST - A photo from WYMT news showed flood waters from the North Fork of the Kentucky River inundating North Main Street near the intersection with Y Street in Hazard.||Multiple other creeks and streams that fed into the North Fork of the Kentucky River also experienced extensive flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between July 25th and July 30th, 2022, several complexes of training thunderstorms developed south of I-64 and brought heavy rain, deadly flash flooding, and devastating river flooding to eastern Kentucky and central Appalachia. These thunderstorms, at times, caused rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr across complex terrain that led to widespread devastating impacts. While it did not rain continuously during this 4-day stretch, the overwhelming amounts of rain and resultant flooding led to 39 deaths and widespread catastrophic damage. Entire homes and parts of some communities were swept away by flood waters, leading to costly damage to infrastructure in the region. Over 600 helicopter rescues and countless swift water rescues by boat were needed to evacuate people who were trapped by the quickly-rising flood waters. In total, 24 Flash Flood Warnings were issued between July 26th and July 30th. Between the evening of July 27th and the mid-morning hours on July 28th (the peak of the event), 13 warnings were issued, 3 of which were upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency.||Radar-based rainfall estimates suggest that upwards of 14-16 of rain fell during this 5-day period in a narrow swath, with many more locations receiving 6-10 of rain. Most of this rain fell during the night of July 27th into the morning of July 28th, which is when the most devastating impacts were felt. The highest totals occurred across an axis that stretches from northern Clay and southern Owsley counties, east through southern Breathitt and northern Leslie counties, into Perry, Knott and Letcher counties. The highest rainfall total report was from southern Knott County, where 14.00 fell between July 25th and July 29th. This site, a cooperative (COOP) observer at Carr Creek Lake, reported 6.71 from 7am July 28th to 7am July 29th, following a report of 6.50 at that site on the previous day. Another COOP site in Buckhorn reported 8.00 of rain for the 24-hour period ending 7am on July 28th. The rainfall total in Buckhorn from July 25th to July 29th was 11.76. These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: West Main Street was covered with flood waters at the railroad underpass in Beattyville. Water was also over Lumber Street at the Valero Gas Station.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Red River was running high through Wolfe County. At 09:00 AM EST there was a report that the Red River was flooding across KY-205 at Lee City. At 11:38 AM EST a second report was made of several inches of standing water from the Red River in the Wolfe County Animal Shelter.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm system was in place across the Upper Mississippi Valley, with all attention for severe weather concentrated there for the day. SPC had all of eastern Kentucky in just a general thunderstorm risk, with increasing risk levels as you headed farther northwest towards the main storm system. That being said, with high pressure to our southeast, Kentucky was in a warm and moist airmass with decent SW flow into the state. Showers and thunderstorms developed throughout the day, mainly in an area oriented from SW to NE. These expanded to eastern Kentucky during the late afternoon and evening hours. Storms ended up producing generally isolated wind damage in Perry and Montgomery counties. The heavy rain also caused some urban flooding in Hazard, KY.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water in downtown Hazard caused a vehicle that was driven into the ponded water to stall. The high water may have been the result of poor drainage as rains moved through.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary lingered near the Ohio River through the day. The boundary provided a lifting mechanism for a moist and unstable air mass over eastern Kentucky. Torrential rainfall on top of already saturated soils caused streams to rapidly rise and flash flood at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flood waters peeled up pavement on Climax Rd between 4 and 5 miles outside of Mount Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was in place across the state during the morning hours on May 6th, setting the stage for increased moisture and warm air into the region. By the afternoon, a nearby low pressure system began to occlude, furthering the lift and instability across the state. Showers and thunderstorms developed across much of the region throughout the day, becoming strong to severe by mid-day. Flooding and wind damage was reported throughout the afternoon and into the evening. A tornado was also reported and confirmed near General Burnside in Pulaski County during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least six inches of water was seen flowing over Wolverine Road near the intersection of Highway 30 near Elkatawa.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms broke out across east Kentucky May 26th thanks to a mature low pressure system moving into the Ohio Valley from the west. This system initially pulled a warm front through eastern Kentucky in the evening, and most of the severe convection was associated with the approach and passage of this warm front. A cold front was then dragged across the region overnight and into the morning. Instability was not overly impressive going into this event, around 1000-1500 J/kg of CAPE. However, storms that formed were able to take advantage of the roughly 40 kts of effective shear over the area and organize into more multicellular and linear structures. The limited CAPE profile meant a more limited hail threat, leaving strong to damaging wind gusts as the primary threat, though tornadoes were not able to be ruled out. Even though storm motions were fairly swift, given the abundant moisture associated with this system, there was also a threat for flash flooding if any training set up or an area was hit multiple times. Several warnings, including Severe Thunderstorm, Flash Flood, and Tornado, were issued that evening, mainly across our most western and southwestern counties. Multiple reports of damaging wind leading to downed trees were received across the same areas, along with a couple of reports of water flowing over roadways. There were some wind reports within the Tornado Warnings, but no tornadoes were confirmed in the JKL area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down onto powerlines on Etna Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was situated over Iowa/Illinois/Missouri during the day of the 18th, with a  warm front extended eastward and across and just north of the Bluegrass State. SPC had placed much of our area in a marginal risk of severe weather early in the day, before upgrading to a slight category during a morning update. A secondary low also developed and moved into the state for the afternoon, creating a secondary warm front boundary - along which lift and all modes of severe weather were possible, with the best chances for wind and hail. There was enough low level sheer that an isolated tornado couldn't be ruled out. As a shortwave trough also approached eastern KY throughout the day, along with an MCS to our west, the atmosphere was primed for severe weather to occur across the southeastern portions of the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down off of Sand Lick Road near the community of Sprule, Kentucky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and storms fired during the afternoon and evening of the 19th in the vicinity of a wavering frontal boundary over eastern Kentucky.  High amounts of instability and ample shear favored splitting supercells. While there were many reports of dime to quarter-sized hail, two particularly intense storms produced up to egg-size hail near Jackson and Stanton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public reported hail slightly larger than a quarter (1.00 inch) near Salt Lick Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system moving from Indiana through the upper Ohio Valley dragged a cold front toward the Bluegrass state over the afternoon and evening of May 3rd. Showers and storms fired up across eastern Kentucky in the late afternoon and early evening hours ahead of the front as instability built. A couple of storms became severe late in the evening as the front neared eastern Kentucky's doorstep.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported to have blown down along Campton Road, or KY-11, near the intersection with KY-77 close to the Nada Tunnel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between July 25th and July 30th, 2022, several complexes of training thunderstorms developed south of I-64 and brought heavy rain, deadly flash flooding, and devastating river flooding to eastern Kentucky and central Appalachia. These thunderstorms, at times, caused rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr across complex terrain that led to widespread devastating impacts. While it did not rain continuously during this 4-day stretch, the overwhelming amounts of rain and resultant flooding led to 39 deaths and widespread catastrophic damage. Entire homes and parts of some communities were swept away by flood waters, leading to costly damage to infrastructure in the region. Over 600 helicopter rescues and countless swift water rescues by boat were needed to evacuate people who were trapped by the quickly-rising flood waters. In total, 24 Flash Flood Warnings were issued between July 26th and July 30th. Between the evening of July 27th and the mid-morning hours on July 28th (the peak of the event), 13 warnings were issued, 3 of which were upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency.||Radar-based rainfall estimates suggest that upwards of 14-16 of rain fell during this 5-day period in a narrow swath, with many more locations receiving 6-10 of rain. Most of this rain fell during the night of July 27th into the morning of July 28th, which is when the most devastating impacts were felt. The highest totals occurred across an axis that stretches from northern Clay and southern Owsley counties, east through southern Breathitt and northern Leslie counties, into Perry, Knott and Letcher counties. The highest rainfall total report was from southern Knott County, where 14.00 fell between July 25th and July 29th. This site, a cooperative (COOP) observer at Carr Creek Lake, reported 6.71 from 7am July 28th to 7am July 29th, following a report of 6.50 at that site on the previous day. Another COOP site in Buckhorn reported 8.00 of rain for the 24-hour period ending 7am on July 28th. The rainfall total in Buckhorn from July 25th to July 29th was 11.76. These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary lingered near the Ohio River through the day. The boundary provided a lifting mechanism for a moist and unstable air mass over eastern Kentucky. Torrential rainfall on top of already saturated soils caused streams to rapidly rise and flash flood at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Swiftly flowing water from the Left Fork of Caney Creek was flowing across KY-611 making it impassable. A video on social media showed traffic backed up on either side of the flooded roadway unable to cross and waiting for the water to recede.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was in place across the state during the morning hours on May 6th, setting the stage for increased moisture and warm air into the region. By the afternoon, a nearby low pressure system began to occlude, furthering the lift and instability across the state. Showers and thunderstorms developed across much of the region throughout the day, becoming strong to severe by mid-day. Flooding and wind damage was reported throughout the afternoon and into the evening. A tornado was also reported and confirmed near General Burnside in Pulaski County during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least six inches of water was seen flowing across Highway 1110 along the north of the Kentucky River near Bush Branch.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was situated over Iowa/Illinois/Missouri during the day of the 18th, with a  warm front extended eastward and across and just north of the Bluegrass State. SPC had placed much of our area in a marginal risk of severe weather early in the day, before upgrading to a slight category during a morning update. A secondary low also developed and moved into the state for the afternoon, creating a secondary warm front boundary - along which lift and all modes of severe weather were possible, with the best chances for wind and hail. There was enough low level sheer that an isolated tornado couldn't be ruled out. As a shortwave trough also approached eastern KY throughout the day, along with an MCS to our west, the atmosphere was primed for severe weather to occur across the southeastern portions of the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down by the Dept. of Highway on Sulfer Springs Rd just off KY-192 near the community of Hightop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms broke out across east Kentucky May 26th thanks to a mature low pressure system moving into the Ohio Valley from the west. This system initially pulled a warm front through eastern Kentucky in the evening, and most of the severe convection was associated with the approach and passage of this warm front. A cold front was then dragged across the region overnight and into the morning. Instability was not overly impressive going into this event, around 1000-1500 J/kg of CAPE. However, storms that formed were able to take advantage of the roughly 40 kts of effective shear over the area and organize into more multicellular and linear structures. The limited CAPE profile meant a more limited hail threat, leaving strong to damaging wind gusts as the primary threat, though tornadoes were not able to be ruled out. Even though storm motions were fairly swift, given the abundant moisture associated with this system, there was also a threat for flash flooding if any training set up or an area was hit multiple times. Several warnings, including Severe Thunderstorm, Flash Flood, and Tornado, were issued that evening, mainly across our most western and southwestern counties. Multiple reports of damaging wind leading to downed trees were received across the same areas, along with a couple of reports of water flowing over roadways. There were some wind reports within the Tornado Warnings, but no tornadoes were confirmed in the JKL area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down on KY Highway 789 near Ramseys Point Road. The Highway Department was called to remove the tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and storms fired during the afternoon and evening of the 19th in the vicinity of a wavering frontal boundary over eastern Kentucky.  High amounts of instability and ample shear favored splitting supercells. While there were many reports of dime to quarter-sized hail, two particularly intense storms produced up to egg-size hail near Jackson and Stanton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture on social media showed approximately quarter-sized (1.00 inch) hail at Levi Jackson Wilderness Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a high pressure center sitting east of the state, Kentucky, along with much of the Deep South found themselves in a strong return flow pattern. This consisted of S to SW winds which were able to pump in ample moisture and warm air from the Gulf of Mexico. However, with no boundaries or airmass changes nearby, the flow aloft was not very strong. High moisture and weak flow materialized into slow moving showers and thunderstorms throughout the day on Saturday. As locations saw rain persist throughout the day, isolated instances of flooding and flash flooding were reported across portions of Rockcastle, Powell, Laurel, Jackson, and Estill Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was reported on the road near the beginning of Hummel Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary lingered near the Ohio River through the day. The boundary provided a lifting mechanism for a moist and unstable air mass over eastern Kentucky. Torrential rainfall on top of already saturated soils caused streams to rapidly rise and flash flood at several locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures show flash flood waters surrounding mobile homes along Freedom School Rd.
