EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms broke out across east Kentucky May 26th thanks to a mature low pressure system moving into the Ohio Valley from the west. This system initially pulled a warm front through eastern Kentucky in the evening, and most of the severe convection was associated with the approach and passage of this warm front. A cold front was then dragged across the region overnight and into the morning. Instability was not overly impressive going into this event, around 1000-1500 J/kg of CAPE. However, storms that formed were able to take advantage of the roughly 40 kts of effective shear over the area and organize into more multicellular and linear structures. The limited CAPE profile meant a more limited hail threat, leaving strong to damaging wind gusts as the primary threat, though tornadoes were not able to be ruled out. Even though storm motions were fairly swift, given the abundant moisture associated with this system, there was also a threat for flash flooding if any training set up or an area was hit multiple times. Several warnings, including Severe Thunderstorm, Flash Flood, and Tornado, were issued that evening, mainly across our most western and southwestern counties. Multiple reports of damaging wind leading to downed trees were received across the same areas, along with a couple of reports of water flowing over roadways. There were some wind reports within the Tornado Warnings, but no tornadoes were confirmed in the JKL area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down onto Bluetick Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and storms fired during the afternoon and evening of the 19th in the vicinity of a wavering frontal boundary over eastern Kentucky.  High amounts of instability and ample shear favored splitting supercells. While there were many reports of dime to quarter-sized hail, two particularly intense storms produced up to egg-size hail near Jackson and Stanton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported half dollar-sized (1.25 inch) hail approximately 2 miles west of Hightop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was in place across the state during the morning hours on May 6th, setting the stage for increased moisture and warm air into the region. By the afternoon, a nearby low pressure system began to occlude, furthering the lift and instability across the state. Showers and thunderstorms developed across much of the region throughout the day, becoming strong to severe by mid-day. Flooding and wind damage was reported throughout the afternoon and into the evening. A tornado was also reported and confirmed near General Burnside in Pulaski County during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and onto Town Hill Road, on the Highway 30W side, just outside of Jackson. Report relayed via social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low was situated over Iowa/Illinois/Missouri during the day of the 18th, with a  warm front extended eastward and across and just north of the Bluegrass State. SPC had placed much of our area in a marginal risk of severe weather early in the day, before upgrading to a slight category during a morning update. A secondary low also developed and moved into the state for the afternoon, creating a secondary warm front boundary - along which lift and all modes of severe weather were possible, with the best chances for wind and hail. There was enough low level sheer that an isolated tornado couldn't be ruled out. As a shortwave trough also approached eastern KY throughout the day, along with an MCS to our west, the atmosphere was primed for severe weather to occur across the southeastern portions of the CWA.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down at Couch Baker Lane near Hightop, Kentucky.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between July 25th and July 30th, 2022, several complexes of training thunderstorms developed south of I-64 and brought heavy rain, deadly flash flooding, and devastating river flooding to eastern Kentucky and central Appalachia. These thunderstorms, at times, caused rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr across complex terrain that led to widespread devastating impacts. While it did not rain continuously during this 4-day stretch, the overwhelming amounts of rain and resultant flooding led to 39 deaths and widespread catastrophic damage. Entire homes and parts of some communities were swept away by flood waters, leading to costly damage to infrastructure in the region. Over 600 helicopter rescues and countless swift water rescues by boat were needed to evacuate people who were trapped by the quickly-rising flood waters. In total, 24 Flash Flood Warnings were issued between July 26th and July 30th. Between the evening of July 27th and the mid-morning hours on July 28th (the peak of the event), 13 warnings were issued, 3 of which were upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency.||Radar-based rainfall estimates suggest that upwards of 14-16 of rain fell during this 5-day period in a narrow swath, with many more locations receiving 6-10 of rain. Most of this rain fell during the night of July 27th into the morning of July 28th, which is when the most devastating impacts were felt. The highest totals occurred across an axis that stretches from northern Clay and southern Owsley counties, east through southern Breathitt and northern Leslie counties, into Perry, Knott and Letcher counties. The highest rainfall total report was from southern Knott County, where 14.00 fell between July 25th and July 29th. This site, a cooperative (COOP) observer at Carr Creek Lake, reported 6.71 from 7am July 28th to 7am July 29th, following a report of 6.50 at that site on the previous day. Another COOP site in Buckhorn reported 8.00 of rain for the 24-hour period ending 7am on July 28th. The rainfall total in Buckhorn from July 25th to July 29th was 11.76. These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.

(Same as 10:48 PM report).|11:07 PM EST - Pictures on social media show considerable flash flooding along Bowling Creek Road near Altro and the Breathitt/Perry County Line.|11:36 PM EST - Destructive flash flooind was observed at the Altro Church of God. |12:10 AM EST - Flood waters have washed out culverts and destroyed the road on Joe Little Fork Road in the Canoe area of Breathitt County.|12:15 AM EST - (Same as above) Flash flood waters have reportedly washed out culverts along Joe Little Fork Road. |3:08 AM EST - Joe Little Road reportedly received a second flash flood.|6:00 AM EST - Pictures on social media show multiple homes swept away by flash flood waters along Lost Creek, adjacent to the intersection of KY-15 and Lost Creek-Dice Road.|6:00 AM EST - Picture on social media shows 2 homes caught in flash flood waters at Nobles Landing along Troublesome Creek.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A midlevel shortwave trough was situated over the Upper Midwest Sunday night (31st) into Monday morning (1st). At the surface, a strong occluded low was also present. From this low and occlusion, a warm front extended to the southeast, lifting through the Commonwealth during the same time frame. A MCS developed in the Upper Mississippi Valley and continued to push southeast towards the Commonwealth during the morning hours. This prompted SPC to put portions of northern and eastern KY in a slight risk for severe weather throughout the day. Ironically, we did not see any severe weather out of this system - however, showers that developed during the morning hours ahead of the MCS were a different story. These showers developed over the eastern portion of the state, and impacted areas that were already heavily saturated from the devastating rains that occurred the week before. More so, the convection was moving parallel to the mean flow, which meant that many of these showers continued to train over the same locations for an extended period of time. This prompted flash flood warnings, and led to impacts in Martin, Pike, Letcher, Floyd, and Knott counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was ponding onto the KY-2759 near Hindman. In addition, a mudslide also occurred, and a large tree is down. Power lines are also down in the area as a result. The road has been been closed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices topped 110 degrees across most of western Kentucky in the afternoon. Peak heat indices ranged from 109 at Marion to 113 at Paducah and 115 at Henderson. A hot and humid southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front brought temperatures up to the mid 90's. Dew points were exceptionally high, with readings in the lower 80's at many stations. Early morning temperatures were quite warm. Paducah broke a daily warm low temperature record of 80 degrees.

A prolonged heat wave gripped most of western Kentucky for several days. From the 4th to the 8th, peak afternoon heat indices ranged from 103 to 119 degrees. The exception was the Owensboro and Henderson area, where the dangerous levels of heat lasted only from the 4th until the 6th. The hottest days in western Kentucky were the 5th and 6th, when the heat index peaked between 109 and 119 at all mesonet and airport sites. Local Paducah hospitals reported an increase in emergency room visits for heat-related illnesses. A couple of dozen heat illnesses were reported, including heat cramps, heat exhaustion, and heat stroke. A Salvation Army cooling center in Paducah reported an increase in visits. During this heat wave, a sprawling 500 mb high covered most of the southern states from the southern Plains to the southern Appalachian Mountains. Beneath this large 500 mb high, daily high temperatures frequently soared to near 100 degrees. Night-time temperatures were quite warm. Paducah set a daily record warm low temperature of 80 degrees on the 6th. Paducah achieved four straight days of 110-degree plus heat indices from July 5-8, which was the first occurrence of this since 1987.

A rather large and slow-moving area of thunderstorms moved east across the lower Ohio Valley. The storms produced heavy rainfall and isolated urban flooding. The storms occurred ahead of a cold front that extended from central Indiana southwest to the Ozark Mountains of Arkansas. A very moist and unstable air mass supported the storms. Southwest 850 mb winds increased to over 30 knots, further aiding storm development.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several inches of water covered Interstate 69 at the interchange of Highway 41.

Areas south of a stationary front within the lower Ohio Valley warmed into the mid 80's to the mid 90's. The air mass was very moist, with dew points in the lower to mid 70's. Instability was quite strong, with mixed-layer capes around 3000. Minimal wind shear inhibited thunderstorm organization. A storm pulsed up to severe levels, producing isolated damaging winds. Weak steering winds resulted in slow storm movement and an isolated flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported in the Drakesboro area. Several ditches overflowed, and yards flooded. Some basements were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The hot and humid afternoon of July 6th brought forth strong to severe thunderstorms as a cold front remained parked in close vicinity to the area. Damaging wind gusts associated with these storms resulted in several downed trees and power lines within the Flatwoods and Summit townships.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power lines were blown down in Flatwoods.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees and power lines were knocked down in the town of Summitt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across Kentucky between July 6th and July 8th. The main focus for convection was areas along and just south of a quasi-stationary surface front, initially positioned from west to east across central Indiana on July 6th. Each afternoon, thunderstorms developed in the unstable airmass, gradually pushing south and east before dissipating quickly after loss of daytime heating. On July 6th, a broken line of strong-to-severe thunderstorms crossed the Ohio River from Indiana during the mid-afternoon hours. As the storms pushed across central Kentucky, downdraft CAPE of greater than 1200 J/kg helped damaging wind gusts reach the ground, and several wind damage reports were observed, mainly across north central Kentucky. On July 7th, lesser instability led to the development of garden-variety thunderstorms, with wind damage only being observed in a few of the strongest cells. Finally, on July 8th, clusters of strong and severe thunderstorms produced scattered wind damage across south central Kentucky, with flash flooding also being observed in Russell County due to repeated bursts of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was rushing over Jericho Road, approximately 1.5 miles east of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered intense thunderstorms fired up during the heat of the day across western Kentucky. This activity developed ahead of a weak cold front and in the vicinity of a weak surface low centered over the mid Mississippi Valley. The atmosphere was quite unstable, with mixed-layer capes from 1500 to 2500 J/kg. Wind shear was sufficiently strong for isolated storms to become severe, with damaging wind gusts. Effective bulk shear was around 30 to 35 knots. The storms maintained intensity through the evening and early night-time hours. Weak steering winds aloft contributed to slow storm movement and an isolated flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The public reported tree damage to a garage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated microburst caused extensive damage at a resort community. A small cluster of intense thunderstorms over the northern end of the Land Between The Lakes area was responsible for the microburst. A cold front extended from northwest Indiana across central Illinois and central Missouri. Thunderstorms developed in the warm and moist southwest flow ahead of this front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The community of Kuttawa was struck by a microburst that downed numerous trees. Some of the trees were two to three feet in diameter. Extensive damage occurred at Kuttawa Harbor. Numerous boats and docks were damaged in the harbor. Three docks were extensively damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms that moved through the Ohio Valley overnight reached eastern Kentucky on the afternoon of July 23rd. These storms were held together by hot and muggy conditions permeating ahead of the activity, allowing convection to thrive in Boyd and Lawrence Counties. Damaging wind gusts accompanying these afternoon storms led to downed trees.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local roadways near the town of Princess were blocked by downed trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across Kentucky between July 6th and July 8th. The main focus for convection was areas along and just south of a quasi-stationary surface front, initially positioned from west to east across central Indiana on July 6th. Each afternoon, thunderstorms developed in the unstable airmass, gradually pushing south and east before dissipating quickly after loss of daytime heating. On July 6th, a broken line of strong-to-severe thunderstorms crossed the Ohio River from Indiana during the mid-afternoon hours. As the storms pushed across central Kentucky, downdraft CAPE of greater than 1200 J/kg helped damaging wind gusts reach the ground, and several wind damage reports were observed, mainly across north central Kentucky. On July 7th, lesser instability led to the development of garden-variety thunderstorms, with wind damage only being observed in a few of the strongest cells. Finally, on July 8th, clusters of strong and severe thunderstorms produced scattered wind damage across south central Kentucky, with flash flooding also being observed in Russell County due to repeated bursts of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down along Leitchfield Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front which pushed through Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on July 12th. Immediately ahead of the front, temperatures in the upper 80s and lower 90s combined with dewpoints in the mid-70s to facilitate the development of greater than 3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE. With plenty of fuel to work with, storms grew rapidly, especially across south central Kentucky, leading to isolated wind damage reports in association with the strongest cells.||On July 14th, the mid-level trough which brought the cold front and associated thunderstorms through Kentucky two days prior began to shift off to the east. A weak surface frontal boundary developed over portions of southwestern Kentucky, and frontal convergence along with modest instability helped contribute to the development of scattered strong thunderstorms across southern and central Kentucky. One of these storms was just strong enough to produce scattered wind damage across Grayson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down on a property along Dripping Springs Church Road near Wisdom.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved from southeastern Missouri into western Kentucky during the late evening hours of July 17th. A combination of modest instability and shear (1500 J/kg MLCAPE; 30 knots effective shear) along with the dynamics of the MCS itself helped to sustain convection well after sunset as the system approached the Natcher Parkway. In addition to heavy rainfall and lightning, a few reports of wind damage were observed in Ohio County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn blew over, and trees blew down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening and overnight hours of July 20th into July 21st, strong to severe thunderstorms developed out ahead of an approaching cold front. On the afternoon of July 20th, temperatures surged into the mid-90s across central Kentucky, with dewpoint temperatures reaching the mid-70s. The warm and unstable low levels, combined with mid-level height falls from an approaching trough, allowed for greater than 4000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE across portions of south central Kentucky during the evening hours of July 20th. Despite some boundary layer stabilization during the late evening hours, a low-level jet allowed for convection to sustain itself through the overnight hours, with scattered wind damage being observed mainly across south central Kentucky. While convection was initially fairly discrete in nature, after midnight, an evolution toward one large convective complex was observed, which contributed to additional flash flooding issues across south central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Lawson Bottom Road were washed out from flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another period of hazardous heat occurred across western Kentucky. Heat indices peaked around 105 degrees along and north of a line from Madisonville to Paducah on the 22nd. Heat indices peaked around 105 degrees everywhere on the 23rd and 24th. The peak heat index at Paducah was 106 on the 22nd, 104 on the 23rd, and 108 on the 24th. Another large 500 mb high became stubbornly persistent across the southern states, from the Four Corners region to the Southeast. Underneath this high, daily high temperatures were in the mid 90's. Overnight lows were mostly in the mid 70's.

A weak disturbance crossed over northeast Kentucky on July 31st at the same time a warm front migrated northward at the surface. These two features provided the needed support for showers and thunderstorms that developed in the late morning time frame. Flash flooding resulted in Boyd County with blocked roadways and a quick response of local creeks.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Rockdale, Shopes Creek rose above its banks and spilled onto Route 538, restricting traffic through the area. Portions of Route 60 near Cannonsburg were also inundated with high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the mid-to-late afternoon hours on July 27th, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed immediately ahead of a quasi-stationary surface frontal boundary located near the Ohio River. Across central Kentucky, temperatures in the upper 80s and lower 90s and dewpoints in the mid 70s provided copious amounts of instability, with convection having mixed-layer CAPE in excess of 3000 J/kg to work with. During the mid-afternoon period, showers and thunderstorms developed along the Western Kentucky and Bluegrass Parkways and moved steadily to the south and east. The strongest storms produced isolated wind damage, mainly across south central Kentucky. While most of the severe threat subsided during the overnight hours, persistent convection across south central Kentucky created isolated flash flooding issues.||During the evening hours of July 28th, a secondary weak frontal boundary helped to spark convection across portions of Kentucky, causing isolated wind damage in Meade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few county roads were flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A small cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along a weak cold front as it pushed southeast across western Kentucky. A few of the storms produced wind gusts around 60 mph. A slow-moving thunderstorm caused isolated flash flooding of an urban area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 38-year-old man who was flying a remote-controlled plane was struck by lightning. He was airlifted to an out-of-state hospital, where he died several days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heat wave gripped most of western Kentucky for several days. From the 4th to the 8th, peak afternoon heat indices ranged from 103 to 119 degrees. The exception was the Owensboro and Henderson area, where the dangerous levels of heat lasted only from the 4th until the 6th. The hottest days in western Kentucky were the 5th and 6th, when the heat index peaked between 109 and 119 at all mesonet and airport sites. Local Paducah hospitals reported an increase in emergency room visits for heat-related illnesses. A couple of dozen heat illnesses were reported, including heat cramps, heat exhaustion, and heat stroke. A Salvation Army cooling center in Paducah reported an increase in visits. During this heat wave, a sprawling 500 mb high covered most of the southern states from the southern Plains to the southern Appalachian Mountains. Beneath this large 500 mb high, daily high temperatures frequently soared to near 100 degrees. Night-time temperatures were quite warm. Paducah set a daily record warm low temperature of 80 degrees on the 6th. Paducah achieved four straight days of 110-degree plus heat indices from July 5-8, which was the first occurrence of this since 1987.

Extreme drought conditions developed along the Tennessee border into the Murray and Mayfield areas of west Kentucky. Severe drought crept northeast across the remainder of far western Kentucky, as far northeast as the Land Between The Lakes area. Crops showed signs of stress. Some farmers were feeding hay to supplement where pastures were struggling. Fire danger increased, and burn bans were imposed in the cities of Murray, Marion, and Hardin. Between 40 and 60 percent of topsoil moisture was considered short or very short.

Numerous showers and storms fired during the afternoon and evening of the 19th in the vicinity of a wavering frontal boundary over eastern Kentucky.  High amounts of instability and ample shear favored splitting supercells. While there were many reports of dime to quarter-sized hail, two particularly intense storms produced up to egg-size hail near Jackson and Stanton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public report on social media indicated approximately nickel-sized (0.88 inch) hail near Sand Gap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across Kentucky between July 6th and July 8th. The main focus for convection was areas along and just south of a quasi-stationary surface front, initially positioned from west to east across central Indiana on July 6th. Each afternoon, thunderstorms developed in the unstable airmass, gradually pushing south and east before dissipating quickly after loss of daytime heating. On July 6th, a broken line of strong-to-severe thunderstorms crossed the Ohio River from Indiana during the mid-afternoon hours. As the storms pushed across central Kentucky, downdraft CAPE of greater than 1200 J/kg helped damaging wind gusts reach the ground, and several wind damage reports were observed, mainly across north central Kentucky. On July 7th, lesser instability led to the development of garden-variety thunderstorms, with wind damage only being observed in a few of the strongest cells. Finally, on July 8th, clusters of strong and severe thunderstorms produced scattered wind damage across south central Kentucky, with flash flooding also being observed in Russell County due to repeated bursts of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were multiple reports of trees and power lines down around downtown Louisville including 18th and Market Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A prolonged heat wave gripped most of western Kentucky for several days. From the 4th to the 8th, peak afternoon heat indices ranged from 103 to 119 degrees. The exception was the Owensboro and Henderson area, where the dangerous levels of heat lasted only from the 4th until the 6th. The hottest days in western Kentucky were the 5th and 6th, when the heat index peaked between 109 and 119 at all mesonet and airport sites. Local Paducah hospitals reported an increase in emergency room visits for heat-related illnesses. A couple of dozen heat illnesses were reported, including heat cramps, heat exhaustion, and heat stroke. A Salvation Army cooling center in Paducah reported an increase in visits. During this heat wave, a sprawling 500 mb high covered most of the southern states from the southern Plains to the southern Appalachian Mountains. Beneath this large 500 mb high, daily high temperatures frequently soared to near 100 degrees. Night-time temperatures were quite warm. Paducah set a daily record warm low temperature of 80 degrees on the 6th. Paducah achieved four straight days of 110-degree plus heat indices from July 5-8, which was the first occurrence of this since 1987.

During the mid-to-late afternoon hours on July 27th, scattered showers and thunderstorms developed immediately ahead of a quasi-stationary surface frontal boundary located near the Ohio River. Across central Kentucky, temperatures in the upper 80s and lower 90s and dewpoints in the mid 70s provided copious amounts of instability, with convection having mixed-layer CAPE in excess of 3000 J/kg to work with. During the mid-afternoon period, showers and thunderstorms developed along the Western Kentucky and Bluegrass Parkways and moved steadily to the south and east. The strongest storms produced isolated wind damage, mainly across south central Kentucky. While most of the severe threat subsided during the overnight hours, persistent convection across south central Kentucky created isolated flash flooding issues.||During the evening hours of July 28th, a secondary weak frontal boundary helped to spark convection across portions of Kentucky, causing isolated wind damage in Meade County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: There were two trees down along Liberty Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices topped 110 degrees across most of western Kentucky in the afternoon. Peak heat indices ranged from 109 at Marion to 113 at Paducah and 115 at Henderson. A hot and humid southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front brought temperatures up to the mid 90's. Dew points were exceptionally high, with readings in the lower 80's at many stations. Early morning temperatures were quite warm. Paducah broke a daily warm low temperature record of 80 degrees.

A couple of dozen heat illnesses were reported, including heat cramps, heat exhaustion, and heat stroke. A Salvation Army cooling center in Paducah reported an increase in visits. During this heat wave, a sprawling 500 mb high covered most of the southern states from the southern Plains to the southern Appalachian Mountains. Beneath this large 500 mb high, daily high temperatures frequently soared to near 100 degrees. Night-time temperatures were quite warm. Paducah set a daily record warm low temperature of 80 degrees on the 6th. Paducah achieved four straight days of 110-degree plus heat indices from July 5-8, which was the first occurrence of this since 1987.

Areas south of a stationary front within the lower Ohio Valley warmed into the mid 80's to the mid 90's. The air mass was very moist, with dew points in the lower to mid 70's. Instability was quite strong, with mixed-layer capes around 3000. Minimal wind shear inhibited thunderstorm organization. A storm pulsed up to severe levels, producing isolated damaging winds. Weak steering winds resulted in slow storm movement and an isolated flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were blown down. One tree was down across Highway 601 in the Luzerne area. A couple other trees were down in Greenville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered intense thunderstorms fired up during the heat of the day across western Kentucky. This activity developed ahead of a weak cold front and in the vicinity of a weak surface low centered over the mid Mississippi Valley. The atmosphere was quite unstable, with mixed-layer capes from 1500 to 2500 J/kg. Wind shear was sufficiently strong for isolated storms to become severe, with damaging wind gusts. Effective bulk shear was around 30 to 35 knots. The storms maintained intensity through the evening and early night-time hours. Weak steering winds aloft contributed to slow storm movement and an isolated flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree tops were broken off south of Highway 453, just a couple miles north of the Tennessee River near the Marshall County border.

Multiple rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms moved across Kentucky between July 6th and July 8th. The main focus for convection was areas along and just south of a quasi-stationary surface front, initially positioned from west to east across central Indiana on July 6th. Each afternoon, thunderstorms developed in the unstable airmass, gradually pushing south and east before dissipating quickly after loss of daytime heating. On July 6th, a broken line of strong-to-severe thunderstorms crossed the Ohio River from Indiana during the mid-afternoon hours. As the storms pushed across central Kentucky, downdraft CAPE of greater than 1200 J/kg helped damaging wind gusts reach the ground, and several wind damage reports were observed, mainly across north central Kentucky. On July 7th, lesser instability led to the development of garden-variety thunderstorms, with wind damage only being observed in a few of the strongest cells. Finally, on July 8th, clusters of strong and severe thunderstorms produced scattered wind damage across south central Kentucky, with flash flooding also being observed in Russell County due to repeated bursts of heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were down on the 8000 block of Todds Point Road.

During the late morning and afternoon hours of July 2nd, clusters of showers and thunderstorms developed along and south of a quasi-stationary frontal boundary which was draped from west to east across Kentucky. South of the boundary, warm and humid conditions in the low levels contributed to sufficient instability for the development of thunderstorms. The most favorable environmental shear was offset from the greatest instability, limiting the overall severe threat, with only one wind damage report observed in Butler County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell on Rochester Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed along a cold front which pushed through Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours on July 12th. Immediately ahead of the front, temperatures in the upper 80s and lower 90s combined with dewpoints in the mid-70s to facilitate the development of greater than 3000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE. With plenty of fuel to work with, storms grew rapidly, especially across south central Kentucky, leading to isolated wind damage reports in association with the strongest cells.||On July 14th, the mid-level trough which brought the cold front and associated thunderstorms through Kentucky two days prior began to shift off to the east. A weak surface frontal boundary developed over portions of southwestern Kentucky, and frontal convergence along with modest instability helped contribute to the development of scattered strong thunderstorms across southern and central Kentucky. One of these storms was just strong enough to produce scattered wind damage across Grayson County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees fell on B. Bertram Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system moved from southeastern Missouri into western Kentucky during the late evening hours of July 17th. A combination of modest instability and shear (1500 J/kg MLCAPE; 30 knots effective shear) along with the dynamics of the MCS itself helped to sustain convection well after sunset as the system approached the Natcher Parkway. In addition to heavy rainfall and lightning, a few reports of wind damage were observed in Ohio County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were down near the intersection of West 7th Street and Ridgecrest Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening and overnight hours of July 20th into July 21st, strong to severe thunderstorms developed out ahead of an approaching cold front. On the afternoon of July 20th, temperatures surged into the mid-90s across central Kentucky, with dewpoint temperatures reaching the mid-70s. The warm and unstable low levels, combined with mid-level height falls from an approaching trough, allowed for greater than 4000 J/kg mixed-layer CAPE across portions of south central Kentucky during the evening hours of July 20th. Despite some boundary layer stabilization during the late evening hours, a low-level jet allowed for convection to sustain itself through the overnight hours, with scattered wind damage being observed mainly across south central Kentucky. While convection was initially fairly discrete in nature, after midnight, an evolution toward one large convective complex was observed, which contributed to additional flash flooding issues across south central Kentucky.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported within the city limits of Glasgow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered intense thunderstorms fired up during the heat of the day across western Kentucky. This activity developed ahead of a weak cold front and in the vicinity of a weak surface low centered over the mid Mississippi Valley. The atmosphere was quite unstable, with mixed-layer capes from 1500 to 2500 J/kg. Wind shear was sufficiently strong for isolated storms to become severe, with damaging wind gusts. Effective bulk shear was around 30 to 35 knots. The storms maintained intensity through the evening and early night-time hours. Weak steering winds aloft contributed to slow storm movement and an isolated flash flood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices topped 110 degrees across most of western Kentucky in the afternoon. Peak heat indices ranged from 109 at Marion to 113 at Paducah and 115 at Henderson. A hot and humid southwest wind flow ahead of a cold front brought temperatures up to the mid 90's. Dew points were exceptionally high, with readings in the lower 80's at many stations. Early morning temperatures were quite warm. Paducah broke a daily warm low temperature record of 80 degrees.

A prolonged heat wave gripped most of western Kentucky for several days. From the 4th to the 8th, peak afternoon heat indices ranged from 103 to 119 degrees. The exception was the Owensboro and Henderson area, where the dangerous levels of heat lasted only from the 4th until the 6th. The hottest days in western Kentucky were the 5th and 6th, when the heat index peaked between 109 and 119 at all mesonet and airport sites. Local Paducah hospitals reported an increase in emergency room visits for heat-related illnesses. A couple of dozen heat illnesses were reported, including heat cramps, heat exhaustion, and heat stroke. A Salvation Army cooling center in Paducah reported an increase in visits. During this heat wave, a sprawling 500 mb high covered most of the southern states from the southern Plains to the southern Appalachian Mountains. Beneath this large 500 mb high, daily high temperatures frequently soared to near 100 degrees. Night-time temperatures were quite warm. Paducah set a daily record warm low temperature of 80 degrees on the 6th. Paducah achieved four straight days of 110-degree plus heat indices from July 5-8, which was the first occurrence of this since 1987.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure center was parked over the northern half of Indiana throughout much of the day on the 17th, with points to the south, including KY, exhibiting a warming and moistening trend throughout the day. Scattered showers and storms were present across Kentucky and surrounding areas throughout the afternoon and evening. A couple of these storms ended up resulting in several downed trees. Multiple rounds of heavy rain moved across portions of Pike County, leading to a flash flood near the community of Mouthcard.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down and onto Barnes Mountain Road southeast of Ravenna.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled near the Ohio Valley on the evening of July 20th. Unusually high instability ahead of the front fueled thunderstorm development late in the evening over southcentral Kentucky just ahead of the front. Moderate shear helped organize the storms as they shifted eastward and evolved into a line of quick-moving storms. The strongest winds occurred from Breathitt County southeastward to the Virginia/Kentucky border during the early morning hours of July 21st. Storm backbuilding and training occurred along the southern edge of the line, leading to substantial rainfall along the Virginia border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree blown down onto a power line approximately 1 mile southwest of Steubenville leading to local power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary boundary was elongated from west to east across the state on the 7th. While the more widespread showers and thunderstorms were located in southern Indiana crossing into north-central Kentucky during the early evening, there were some additional stronger showers and thunderstorms loacted across the southeast of the state as well. One of these storms went through McCreary County, resulting in multiple trees being downed in and around the Strunk community.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down in and around Strunk.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Kentucky was experiencing warm and moist SW flow heading through the day on July 5, while flow in the mid and upper levels was more westerly. While no forcing mechanism was in place across the state )as far as low pressure center, shortwave, or frontal boundary) and the better severe weather concerns were concentrated well to our NW, the low level wind sheer and decent Cape values were enough to create good boyancy and spawn showers and thunderstorms throughout the afternoon and evening. The main concern with these storms was strong winds being transferred down to the surface, which did result in multiple instances of downed trees. Heavy rain that impacted Clay County and Montgomery County also lead to a swift water rescue and isolated instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on US-460 in the community of Shelbiana.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary and surface low pressure south of the state early Sunday morning began to slowly lift northward into the southeast and south-central portion of the state throughout the day. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the boundary and moved eastward. Since the system as whole showed very little forward progression, multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms ended up moving across the same locations multiple times, on top of already saturated soils. This ended up leading to multiple instances of flash flooding, specifically in Whitley and Pike Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Whitley County 911 dispatch reported that a water rescue took place along Little Wolf Creek Road near Mountain Ash.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low situated over northern New England continued to push northeastward throughout the day, dragging a frontal boundary with it. This boundary became east-west oriented and stalled across the northern Ohio Valley throughout the day, placing Kentucky and areas southward in a warm and humid (unstable) air mass. Showers and storms developed during the afternoon hours across portions of the southern Ohio Valley, eventually leading to a MCS which traveled southeastward and into the Jackson forecast area by the evening. Winds were the main concern with storms throughout the day, especially along the MCS. SPC included much of central and eastern Kentucky in a Slight Risk for severe storms with wind being the main threat. This came to fruition as storms ended up producing widespread wind damage across portions of the southeast Ohio Valley and Mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A ree reported down at the corner of Spout Springs Rd and Hardwicks Creek Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Upper Midwest was under the gun on July 23rd, with SPC forecasting damaging thunderstorm wind gusts (some of 75 mph), very large hail, and a few tornadoes. As convection was ongoing well to our northwest, diurnally driven showers and thunderstorms had popped up across eastern KY amid a warm and moist airmass in place. WPC analyzed an outflow boundary dropping south across the Ohio Valley from the larger area of main convection. By the time it made it into eastern KY, this likely served as a extra boundary to lead to destabalization and strengthening of a couple of the storms. Several localized thunderstorm wind reports were made before storms eventually fizzled out heading into the ovenright hours with loss of daytime forcing.||An area of storms northeast of the state during the morning hours eventually made their way southeast and into eastern Kentucky by the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public video showed a tree down on a powerline around 2 miles southwest of Dorton.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another round of heavy rain showers impacted portions of eastern Kentucky during the early morning hours of July 27th, thanks to a lingering and weakening stationary boundary across the region and a strong flow of moisture advection on the heals of SW flow. Several of these heavy rain producers went across portions of Pike County, leading to two reports of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pike County dispatch reported that Indian Creek Road past Sunny Fork was closed due to high water.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous showers and storms fired during the afternoon and evening of the 19th in the vicinity of a wavering frontal boundary over eastern Kentucky.  High amounts of instability and ample shear favored splitting supercells. While there were many reports of dime to quarter-sized hail, two particularly intense storms produced up to egg-size hail near Jackson and Stanton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The county emergency manager reported a storm producing half dollar-sized (1.25 inch) hail at Hightop.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern Kentucky during the day on Tuesday July 12. It then slowed and eventually stalled across eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours. With most of the area seeing plenty of time for heating and destabalization ahead of the front, the presence of the boundary across the region was the added lift needed to spawn strong thunderstorms that resulted in multiple instances of wind damage, mainly across McCreary and Bell Counties. Pulaski County also experienced some localized damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front was in place across the state during the morning hours on May 6th, setting the stage for increased moisture and warm air into the region. By the afternoon, a nearby low pressure system began to occlude, furthering the lift and instability across the state. Showers and thunderstorms developed across much of the region throughout the day, becoming strong to severe by mid-day. Flooding and wind damage was reported throughout the afternoon and into the evening. A tornado was also reported and confirmed near General Burnside in Pulaski County during the early afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: NWS verified an EF-1 tornado that touched down on Hardwick Road in Bronston, and moved northeast, lifting in the community of Antioch. Two injuries were sustained when a mobile home was flipped on Hardwick Road. The tornado had maximum winds of 110 mph with a path length of just over 3 miles and a path width of 100 yards. A video of the tornado 1 mile NNW of Tateville was also posted on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stalled frontal boundary and surface low pressure south of the state early Sunday morning began to slowly lift northward into the southeast and south-central portion of the state throughout the day. Showers and thunderstorms developed along the boundary and moved eastward. Since the system as whole showed very little forward progression, multiple rounds of showers and thunderstorms ended up moving across the same locations multiple times, on top of already saturated soils. This ended up leading to multiple instances of flash flooding, specifically in Whitley and Pike Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Six inches of water was observed flowing over Hwy 1804 at the I-75 overpass near Fairview.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled near the Ohio Valley on the evening of July 20th. Unusually high instability ahead of the front fueled thunderstorm development late in the evening over southcentral Kentucky just ahead of the front. Moderate shear helped organize the storms as they shifted eastward and evolved into a line of quick-moving storms. The strongest winds occurred from Breathitt County southeastward to the Virginia/Kentucky border during the early morning hours of July 21st. Storm backbuilding and training occurred along the southern edge of the line, leading to substantial rainfall along the Virginia border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large limbs were blown down onto KY-30 near Noctor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Between July 25th and July 30th, 2022, several complexes of training thunderstorms developed south of I-64 and brought heavy rain, deadly flash flooding, and devastating river flooding to eastern Kentucky and central Appalachia. These thunderstorms, at times, caused rainfall rates in excess of 4/hr across complex terrain that led to widespread devastating impacts. While it did not rain continuously during this 4-day stretch, the overwhelming amounts of rain and resultant flooding led to 39 deaths and widespread catastrophic damage. Entire homes and parts of some communities were swept away by flood waters, leading to costly damage to infrastructure in the region. Over 600 helicopter rescues and countless swift water rescues by boat were needed to evacuate people who were trapped by the quickly-rising flood waters. In total, 24 Flash Flood Warnings were issued between July 26th and July 30th. Between the evening of July 27th and the mid-morning hours on July 28th (the peak of the event), 13 warnings were issued, 3 of which were upgraded to a Flash Flood Emergency.||Radar-based rainfall estimates suggest that upwards of 14-16 of rain fell during this 5-day period in a narrow swath, with many more locations receiving 6-10 of rain. Most of this rain fell during the night of July 27th into the morning of July 28th, which is when the most devastating impacts were felt. The highest totals occurred across an axis that stretches from northern Clay and southern Owsley counties, east through southern Breathitt and northern Leslie counties, into Perry, Knott and Letcher counties. The highest rainfall total report was from southern Knott County, where 14.00 fell between July 25th and July 29th. This site, a cooperative (COOP) observer at Carr Creek Lake, reported 6.71 from 7am July 28th to 7am July 29th, following a report of 6.50 at that site on the previous day. Another COOP site in Buckhorn reported 8.00 of rain for the 24-hour period ending 7am on July 28th. The rainfall total in Buckhorn from July 25th to July 29th was 11.76. These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive flash flooding occurred in northwest Perry County. Some of the reports include:||12:00 AM EST - Water rescues were performed on Cam Johnson Branch and Freeman Fork Road in the Buckhorn community.|12:00 AM EST - Water rescues were performed on Garfield Lane and Keneva Road in the Chavies community.|12:00 AM EST - Public reported a house is cascading into a creek, split in half. |12:00 AM EST - Pictures on social media show mud and debris along the floor at the Davidson Church along KY-28. |12:00 AM EST - Severe flash flood damage and bridge washed out near the intersection of Buck Branch Road and KY-28 near Chavies. |2:27 AM EST - Pictures on social media show significant flash flooding through Buckhorn School. |2:40 AM EST - Buckhorn School is flooded and nearby Squabble Creek is out of its banks.|6:00 AM EST - The cooperative observer at Buckhorn Lake reported 8 inches of rainfall in a 24 hour period.

These rainfall values occurring in such a short period of time are incredibly rare: there is less than a 1 in 1000 chance for this much rainfall over five days in a given year.||The incredible rainfall also led to significant rises and flooding on many rivers in eastern Kentucky including on the main stem of the Kentucky River; North, Middle and South forks of the Kentucky River; Red River and Licking River. At Whitesburg, the North Fork of the Kentucky River swelled well above major flood stage, reaching close to 21' before gauge failure (the previous flood of record was 14.7' in 1957). The North Fork of the Kentucky River at Jackson also reached major flood, setting a new record crest of 43.47' (the previous record was 43.1' set in 1939). The river flooding caused a second round of destruction for communities in the region, and caused flooding in downstream areas that did not receive as much rainfall.||*Note* Given the widespread nature and severity of this event - any dollar amount estimated by storm data will not do the event as a whole justice. Since eastern KY was a national declaration, FEMA will be doing a full cost assessment. Will use this data once available for total monetary amounts of damage.||Visit www.weather.gov/jkl/July2022Flooding for more information on the event, as well as pictures and videos of the flooding and damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures on social media show minor flash flooding and debris along Page Cutoff Road near the community of Calvin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approached eastern Kentucky during the day on Tuesday July 12. It then slowed and eventually stalled across eastern Kentucky during the afternoon and evening hours. With most of the area seeing plenty of time for heating and destabalization ahead of the front, the presence of the boundary across the region was the added lift needed to spawn strong thunderstorms that resulted in multiple instances of wind damage, mainly across McCreary and Bell Counties. Pulaski County also experienced some localized damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down near the intersection of KY-1651 and Old US-27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front stalled near the Ohio Valley on the evening of July 20th. Unusually high instability ahead of the front fueled thunderstorm development late in the evening over southcentral Kentucky just ahead of the front. Moderate shear helped organize the storms as they shifted eastward and evolved into a line of quick-moving storms. The strongest winds occurred from Breathitt County southeastward to the Virginia/Kentucky border during the early morning hours of July 21st. Storm backbuilding and training occurred along the southern edge of the line, leading to substantial rainfall along the Virginia border.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Station KY-PL-8 reported hail up to quarter-size (1.00 inch) approximately 4.2 miles south southeast of Burnside.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A stationary front with a moist airmass was situated over the forecast area. As storms developed along the boundary in the afternoon, heavy rainfall combined with training storms moving across the same locations, caused instances of flash flooding across the far eastern portion of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained storm spotter near Bonaza, KY reported that 1.5 to 2 feet of water was flowing down Abbott Creek Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A surface low situated over northern New England continued to push northeastward throughout the day, dragging a frontal boundary with it. This boundary became east-west oriented and stalled across the northern Ohio Valley throughout the day, placing Kentucky and areas southward in a warm and humid (unstable) air mass. Showers and storms developed during the afternoon hours across portions of the southern Ohio Valley, eventually leading to a MCS which traveled southeastward and into the Jackson forecast area by the evening. Winds were the main concern with storms throughout the day, especially along the MCS. SPC included much of central and eastern Kentucky in a Slight Risk for severe storms with wind being the main threat. This came to fruition as storms ended up producing widespread wind damage across portions of the southeast Ohio Valley and Mid-Atlantic.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was reported down on KY-66 at mile marker 27.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Kentucky was experiencing warm and moist SW flow heading through the day on July 5, while flow in the mid and upper levels was more westerly. While no forcing mechanism was in place across the state )as far as low pressure center, shortwave, or frontal boundary) and the better severe weather concerns were concentrated well to our NW, the low level wind sheer and decent Cape values were enough to create good boyancy and spawn showers and thunderstorms throughout the afternoon and evening. The main concern with these storms was strong winds being transferred down to the surface, which did result in multiple instances of downed trees. Heavy rain that impacted Clay County and Montgomery County also lead to a swift water rescue and isolated instances of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Route 11 was closed due to water over the road. Water was on it's way down at the time of the report.

Winds were the main concern with storms throughout the day, especially along the MCS. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Upper Midwest was under the gun on July 23rd, with SPC forecasting damaging thunderstorm wind gusts (some of 75 mph), very large hail, and a few tornadoes. As convection was ongoing well to our northwest, diurnally driven showers and thunderstorms had popped up across eastern KY amid a warm and moist airmass in place. WPC analyzed an outflow boundary dropping south across the Ohio Valley from the larger area of main convection. By the time it made it into eastern KY, this likely served as a extra boundary to lead to destabalization and strengthening of a couple of the storms. Several localized thunderstorm wind reports were made before storms eventually fizzled out heading into the ovenright hours with loss of daytime forcing.||An area of storms northeast of the state during the morning hours eventually made their way southeast and into eastern Kentucky by the late afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees were blown down on Route 149 near the community of Eriline.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours on September 3rd, a combination of ample moisture and sufficient instability provided the necessary ingredients for widespread convection, resulting in locally heavy rainfall and isolated wind damage reports. On the synoptic scale, a weak mid-level disturbance was located across the mid-Mississippi Valley, with a surface low located across western Kentucky. Ahead of this disturbance, a moist air mass was located across central Kentucky, with Precipitable Water values approaching, and in some locations, exceeding 2 inches during the afternoon hours on the 3rd. SBCAPE in excess of 2000 J/kg was sufficient for widespread convection, and while lapse rates and DCAPE were not particularly suggestive of widespread damaging winds, isolated wind damage was observed with the strongest storms, located across Simpson and Warren County. While severe weather was not prevalent across north central Kentucky, multiple waves of heavy rainfall from relatively slow-moving showers and thunderstorms resulted in flash flooding, particularly across Trimble, Shelby, and Franklin County. Multiple reports of road closures and damage to homes along rivers and streams were reported in the aforementioned areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were reported down on Gold City Road near Franklin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A band of thunderstorms containing rainfall rates up to two inches per hour trained over parts of northwest Kentucky. The storms produced some urban flash flooding. The storms intensified during the heat of the day ahead of a 500 mb shortwave trough drifting eastward toward the Ohio River Valley. A tropical air mass was firmly entrenched, with precipitable water values well above the 90th percentile for early September. Effective vertical wind shear from 20 to 25 knots resulted in loosely organized bands of storms. The mean steering flow was parallel to the synoptic forcing, which caused training of the bands of storms with heavy rainfall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Highway 41 in the retail area on the east side of Henderson.

A residential neighborhood in the immediate vicinity of this gage was flooded. There was also a report of 5.37 inches a mile west of the Owensboro airport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unique cool season severe weather event took place across central Kentucky during the evening hours on November 29th. An approaching trough aloft and cold front at the surface created a strongly forced environment across the Ohio and Tennessee valleys. A 50 knot 925-mb jet and a 60 knot 850-mb jet provided plenty of vertical wind shear, with between 50 and 60 knots of effective bulk shear present across central Kentucky during the evening hours. Instability was relatively unimpressive, as generally between 500 and 1000 J/kg MUCAPE could be found in the pre-frontal environment. However, thanks to the strong environmental wind shear, supercell thunderstorms were able to develop across south central Kentucky during the early evening hours, moving quickly to the northeast after developing. Sufficient CAPE in the -10 to -30 degrees Celsius layer and long, straight hodographs contributed to left splitting supercells which produced several reports of hail across the region, ranging from quarter sized hail to ping pong ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter sized hail was observed near the Henry Clay Estate in Lexington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened again in September after a period of wet weather in August. Moderate drought conditions expanded back into the Purchase area and the Land Between the Lakes area, including all areas along and west of Lake Barkley. Far western Kentucky experienced an abnormally dry month, ranking in the top 10 driest Septembers on record. Many areas from Lake Barkley and Kentucky Lake westward struggled to pick up an inch of rain the entire month. At Paducah, September was tied for the 6th driest September on record. The monthly rainfall at Paducah was 0.66 inch, which resulted in a deficit of 2.89 inches below normal. Field and brush fire danger increased as grass turned brown. Bans on outdoor burning were initiated in several counties by the end of the month. The dryness of the summer as a whole, combined with periods of excessive heat, took a toll on crop yields. The corn crop was more adversely affected than the soybean crop because of the timing of the drought earlier in the summer. Soybean yields were not affected, but corn yields were forecast to be up to 25 percent lower than 2021.

Multiple reports of road closures and damage to homes along rivers and streams were reported in the aforementioned areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down along Blue Level Providence Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions worsened again in September after a period of wet weather in August. Moderate drought conditions expanded back into the Purchase area and the Land Between the Lakes area, including all areas along and west of Lake Barkley. Far western Kentucky experienced an abnormally dry month, ranking in the top 10 driest Septembers on record. Many areas from Lake Barkley and Kentucky Lake westward struggled to pick up an inch of rain the entire month. At Paducah, September was tied for the 6th driest September on record. The monthly rainfall at Paducah was 0.66 inch, which resulted in a deficit of 2.89 inches below normal. Field and brush fire danger increased as grass turned brown. Bans on outdoor burning were initiated in several counties by the end of the month. The dryness of the summer as a whole, combined with periods of excessive heat, took a toll on crop yields. The corn crop was more adversely affected than the soybean crop because of the timing of the drought earlier in the summer. Soybean yields were not affected, but corn yields were forecast to be up to 25 percent lower than 2021.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong south winds gusted from 40 to around 50 mph. The highest measured gust in western Kentucky was 54 mph at the mesonet site near Morganfield. There were several other measured gusts to 50 mph in northwest Kentucky, including 52 mph at Owensboro, 53 mph near Henderson, and 50 mph near Calhoun and Marion. To the south, peak wind gusts included 48 mph near Madisonville and Hopkinsville, 46 mph near Princeton, and 45 mph near Paducah and Elkton. In Paducah, a large section of a tree blew down. The tree crushed a small garage. The strong south winds occurred ahead of a cold front that trailed south from a low pressure center over the upper Mississippi Valley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very early season snowfall blanketed western Kentucky during the early morning hours. The highest accumulations ranged from two to three inches along the Ohio River from Owensboro and Henderson southwest to Paducah and Wickliffe. Less than an inch fell in the Tennessee border counties from Hopkinsville west to Murray and Fulton. Most of the snow fell in just 1 to 3 hours during the early morning hours. Visibility was below one mile in the heaviest snow bands. Because temperatures had been in the 60's, 70's, and even lower 80's in the days preceding the snow, pavement temperatures were such that the snow melted on roads. However, snowfall rates were high enough to briefly cover bridges and overpasses, resulting in a few accidents. This unseasonably early snowfall was the third earliest snow event of an inch or more at Paducah, where 2.3 inches fell. The earliest was on the day before Halloween of 1993 (2 inches), and the second earliest was on November 11, 2019 (1.2 inches). The snow occurred in cold northerly flow behind a strong cold front and ahead of a deep 500 mb trough from the upper Missississippi Valley to the Ozark plateau.

Visibility was below one mile in the heaviest snow bands. Because temperatures had been in the 60's, 70's, and even lower 80's in the days preceding the snow, pavement temperatures were such that the snow melted on roads. However, snowfall rates were high enough to briefly cover bridges and overpasses, resulting in a few accidents. This unseasonably early snowfall was the third earliest snow event of an inch or more at Paducah, where 2.3 inches fell. The earliest was on the day before Halloween of 1993 (2 inches), and the second earliest was on November 11, 2019 (1.2 inches). The snow occurred in cold northerly flow behind a strong cold front and ahead of a deep 500 mb trough from the upper Missississippi Valley to the Ozark plateau.

Drought conditions worsened again in September after a period of wet weather in August. Moderate drought conditions expanded back into the Purchase area and the Land Between the Lakes area, including all areas along and west of Lake Barkley. Far western Kentucky experienced an abnormally dry month, ranking in the top 10 driest Septembers on record. Many areas from Lake Barkley and Kentucky Lake westward struggled to pick up an inch of rain the entire month. At Paducah, September was tied for the 6th driest September on record. The monthly rainfall at Paducah was 0.66 inch, which resulted in a deficit of 2.89 inches below normal. Field and brush fire danger increased as grass turned brown. Bans on outdoor burning were initiated in several counties by the end of the month. The dryness of the summer as a whole, combined with periods of excessive heat, took a toll on crop yields. The corn crop was more adversely affected than the soybean crop because of the timing of the drought earlier in the summer. Soybean yields were not affected, but corn yields were forecast to be up to 25 percent lower than 2021.

A quasi-stationary surface front and weak mid-level cyclone continued to meander across the Ohio and Tennessee valleys on September 5th and 6th, providing localized convergence and broad forcing for ascent across central Kentucky. Temperatures in the low-to-mid 80s and dewpoints in the low 70s facilitated the development of around 2000 J/kg SBCAPE and Precipitable Water values approaching 2 inches during the afternoon and evening hours. The aforementioned thermodynamic and kinematic support was sufficient to promote widespread convection during the afternoon and evening hours on both September 5th and 6th. Relatively stagnant flow throughout most of the depth of the troposphere contributed to slow storm motion, resulting in localized flash flooding where the heaviest showers and thunderstorms developed and persisted. Multiple reports of flooding were observed in Taylor County on the afternoon of the 5th, with flooding being observed in Barren County on the afternoon of the 6th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 527 in northern Taylor County became temporarily impassable due to water over the roadway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A unique cool season severe weather event took place across central Kentucky during the evening hours on November 29th. An approaching trough aloft and cold front at the surface created a strongly forced environment across the Ohio and Tennessee valleys. A 50 knot 925-mb jet and a 60 knot 850-mb jet provided plenty of vertical wind shear, with between 50 and 60 knots of effective bulk shear present across central Kentucky during the evening hours. Instability was relatively unimpressive, as generally between 500 and 1000 J/kg MUCAPE could be found in the pre-frontal environment. However, thanks to the strong environmental wind shear, supercell thunderstorms were able to develop across south central Kentucky during the early evening hours, moving quickly to the northeast after developing. Sufficient CAPE in the -10 to -30 degrees Celsius layer and long, straight hodographs contributed to left splitting supercells which produced several reports of hail across the region, ranging from quarter sized hail to ping pong ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Half dollar sized hail was observed near the Fayette/Bourbon County line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Across the Henderson and Owensboro area, widespread dense fog reduced visibility to one-quarter mile or less during the early morning hours. High pressure extending from the central Gulf coast through the Tennessee and lower Ohio Valleys resulted in conditions favorable for the fog.

Strong south winds gusted from 40 to around 50 mph. The highest measured gust in western Kentucky was 54 mph at the mesonet site near Morganfield. There were several other measured gusts to 50 mph in northwest Kentucky, including 52 mph at Owensboro, 53 mph near Henderson, and 50 mph near Calhoun and Marion. To the south, peak wind gusts included 48 mph near Madisonville and Hopkinsville, 46 mph near Princeton, and 45 mph near Paducah and Elkton. In Paducah, a large section of a tree blew down. The tree crushed a small garage. The strong south winds occurred ahead of a cold front that trailed south from a low pressure center over the upper Mississippi Valley.

Drought conditions remained unchanged or improved slightly during the month. The drought severity improved from extreme to severe in the westernmost counties from Paducah and Mayfield westward. Further east, the drought remained severe across most of the rest of western Kentucky. The exception was the Henderson/Owensboro area, where the drought was classified as moderate. Most crops had been harvested, so agricultural impacts were limited. A local farmer estimated the summer and fall drought resulted in a reduction of 30 to 40 bushels per acre compared to previous yields. However, pastures were in poor or very poor condition. This forced farmers to begin feeding hay to cattle, which is normally reserved for the winter months. Stock ponds ran low or completely dried up. Wildfire danger remained high at times during the month. However, periodic light rainfalls allowed all counties to lift bans on outdoor burning. Even so, rainfall deficits were still 5 to 8 inches below normal for the fall (September 1 to November 30). One of the most significant impacts was very low water levels on the Mississippi and Ohio Rivers. Parts of the Mississippi River reached all-time low levels, however rain improved river levels sufficiently for navigation to fully resume by the end of the month.

Drought conditions remained unchanged or improved slightly during the month. The drought severity improved from extreme to severe in the westernmost counties from Paducah and Mayfield westward. Further east, the drought remained severe across most of the rest of western Kentucky. The exception was the Henderson/Owensboro area, where the drought was classified as moderate. Most crops had been harvested, so agricultural impacts were limited. A local farmer estimated the summer and fall drought resulted in a reduction of 30 to 40 bushels per acre compared to previous yields. However, pastures were in poor or very poor condition. This forced farmers to begin feeding hay to cattle, which is normally reserved for the winter months. Stock ponds ran low or completely dried up. Wildfire danger remained high at times during the month. However, periodic light rainfalls allowed all counties to lift bans on outdoor burning. Even so, rainfall deficits were still 5 to 8 inches below normal for the fall (September 1 to November 30). Parts of the Mississippi River reached all-time low levels, however rain improved river levels sufficiently for navigation to fully resume by the end of the month.

A unique cool season severe weather event took place across central Kentucky during the evening hours on November 29th. An approaching trough aloft and cold front at the surface created a strongly forced environment across the Ohio and Tennessee valleys. A 50 knot 925-mb jet and a 60 knot 850-mb jet provided plenty of vertical wind shear, with between 50 and 60 knots of effective bulk shear present across central Kentucky during the evening hours. Instability was relatively unimpressive, as generally between 500 and 1000 J/kg MUCAPE could be found in the pre-frontal environment. However, thanks to the strong environmental wind shear, supercell thunderstorms were able to develop across south central Kentucky during the early evening hours, moving quickly to the northeast after developing. Sufficient CAPE in the -10 to -30 degrees Celsius layer and long, straight hodographs contributed to left splitting supercells which produced several reports of hail across the region, ranging from quarter sized hail to ping pong ball sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports of hail, ranging from between quarter size to ping pong ball size, were received from the Bardstown area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread dense fog blanketed western Kentucky during the night-time and early morning hours. Visibility was reduced to one-quarter mile or less at times. High pressure centered over southern Illinois produced conditions favorable for dense fog.

Strong south winds gusted from 40 to around 50 mph. The highest measured gust in western Kentucky was 54 mph at the mesonet site near Morganfield. There were several other measured gusts to 50 mph in northwest Kentucky, including 52 mph at Owensboro, 53 mph near Henderson, and 50 mph near Calhoun and Marion. To the south, peak wind gusts included 48 mph near Madisonville and Hopkinsville, 46 mph near Princeton, and 45 mph near Paducah and Elkton. In Paducah, a large section of a tree blew down. The tree crushed a small garage. The strong south winds occurred ahead of a cold front that trailed south from a low pressure center over the upper Mississippi Valley.

A surface low pressure center and its parent 500 mb shortwave moved south-southeast from the northern Plains across the middle Mississippi Valley. An area of snow in advance of these features produced an inch or two of accumulation during the late night and early morning hours. The snow readily accumulated on road surfaces due to the antecedent frigid temperatures the previous few days. Driving conditions were treacherous during the morning commute, but relatively light traffic on the observed Christmas holiday kept traffic mishaps less numerous than they otherwise might have been. One person was injured in a wreck on the Pennyrile Parkway in Christian County. A vehicle slid off the roadway, then three more vehicles collided while trying to avoid emergency responders at the scene of the slide-off. The injured person was in one of the three colliding vehicles.

A quick burst of snow arrived into eastern Kentucky during the early morning hours of December 23rd as a cold front swept through the region. One to two inches of snow was measured by local cooperative observers during this time.

Periods of light freezing drizzle occurred during the late night and early morning hours, resulting in icy road conditions. The freezing drizzle was mainly over the Pennyrile and Kentucky Lake areas, along and east of the counties bordering Kentucky Lake. A multi-vehicle crash occurred on Interstate 24 in Livingston County, between the Tennessee River bridge and the Eddyville exit. Westbound traffic was completely blocked for several hours, while eastbound traffic was restricted to one lane. Two semis were involved in one of the crashes. Elsewhere, there was a crash on the Pennyrile Parkway in Hopkinsville that resulted in one injury. The accident happened on an icy overpass. The freezing drizzle occurred in a region of very moist low-level air with light winds.

A surface low pressure center and its parent 500 mb shortwave moved south-southeast from the northern Plains across the middle Mississippi Valley. An area of snow in advance of these features produced an inch or two of accumulation during the late night and early morning hours. The snow readily accumulated on road surfaces due to the antecedent frigid temperatures the previous few days. Driving conditions were treacherous during the morning commute, but relatively light traffic on the observed Christmas holiday kept traffic mishaps less numerous than they otherwise might have been. One person was injured in a wreck on the Pennyrile Parkway in Christian County. A vehicle slid off the roadway, then three more vehicles collided while trying to avoid emergency responders at the scene of the slide-off. The injured person was in one of the three colliding vehicles.
